chapters.description: He explores how soul-making can involve tension, pathos, and even pain, drawing on James Hillman and Ajahn Chah to keep the issue unsettled.

chapters.title: Suffering and interpretation
chapters.description: He examines how retreat culture, discipline, and ideas of purification or awakening can wrap suffering into fantasy, and how the meanings of dukkha vary.

chapters.title: What divinity means
chapters.description: He distinguishes divinity as something inexhaustible, relational, and beyond full capture, with experiences and concepts both unfolding over time.

title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues his exploration of desire by inviting listeners to question the assumptions that usually shape it: that desire comes from lack, that it is mainly craving, or that it must be simply let go. He develops a quieter alternative in which eros, imagery, and inquiry work together, sometimes turning frustration into spaciousness, peace, and soulmaking. The talk also revisits teacher-fantasy, boundaries, and the way desire can open into a wider sense of world rather than staying fixed on a single object.
short_summary: A detailed inquiry into desire as eros, imaginal perception, and soulmaking rather than mere craving or lack.

chapters.title: Inquiry as invitation
chapters.description: He begins by emphasizing permission to question, experiment, and uncover assumptions for oneself.

chapters.title: Reframing desire
chapters.description: He revisits the earlier practice of opening to a deeper current of desire and how it unsettles common views of selfing and defilement.

chapters.title: Eros and the imaginal
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from craving and shows how imaginal perception can expand what feels stuck or impossible.

chapters.title: Peace without flattening
chapters.description: He argues that allowing eros can bring equanimity and spaciousness without losing soul or depth.

chapters.title: Teacher fantasy and soulmaking
chapters.description: He turns to teacher-student longing, keeps the boundary clear, and uses a student’s letter to show how attraction can reveal a whole world.

chapters.title: Psyche loves itself
chapters.description: He develops the idea that psyche is drawn to its own treasures and that boundaries can form a temenos or alchemical vessel.

chapters.title: Desire beyond lack
chapters.description: He questions the idea that desire comes from deficiency and explores longing, trust, and the possibility that desire is already flowing in the imaginal.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He reviews several practices that transform desire through imaginal perception, including divine influx and hierophantic time.

chapters.title: Alchemy of desire
chapters.description: He closes by distinguishing many forms of desire, then gathers them into an alchemy in which craving can become eros and transubstantiation.

title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea revisits a practice for working with desire that begins by asking what desire really wants, then clearing away default Dharma assumptions about desire, and finally feeling the movement of desire in the body and energy body. He uses personal stories and teaching examples to show how desire can open into something more general and life-giving, rather than collapsing into craving or self-division. Later, he places the practice alongside emotional pain, dependent origination, and his broader work on eros and emptiness, while cautioning that the method is not the same as generic acceptance.
short_summary: A detailed teaching on a three-step way of meeting desire that can reveal a deeper, more spacious current beneath craving.

chapters.title: Introducing the inquiry
chapters.description: He opens by situating the talk as a further exploration of desire and a different approach from what has come before.

chapters.title: Origin story
chapters.description: He расскаts the Gaia House episode where asking what he really wanted revealed a desire for freedom from constraint.

chapters.title: Three-step practice
chapters.description: He sets out the core method: unhook desire from its obvious object, clear preconceptions, and feel the deeper current in the whole being.

chapters.title: Examples of craving
chapters.description: He uses a romance interview and related reflections to show how craving amplifies difference and how deeper desire can be uncovered.

chapters.title: Desire becomes inquiry
chapters.description: A second autobiographical example shows how pressure and impatience around study can open into joy in ongoing inquiry.

chapters.title: Desire and lack
chapters.description: He pauses to note that desire is often mixed with lack and that sometimes distress or grief needs to be met first.

chapters.title: Experiment, don't flatten
chapters.description: He stresses flexibility, warns against reducing the teaching to simple acceptance, and encourages repeated experimentation until the method becomes a usable tool.

chapters.title: Relation to imaginal practice
chapters.description: He closes by contrasting this practice with imaginal work, then widens back out to clinging, dependent origination, and emptiness.

title: Eros and the Brahmaviharas
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the relation between eros and the Brahmaviharas, carefully distinguishing erotic imaginal practice from mettā, compassion, joy, and equanimity while also showing where they overlap. He argues that eros adds desire, beauty, particularity, and complexity, whereas the Brahmaviharas tend toward universality, simplicity, and less fabrication. The talk then turns practical: how erotic imaginal practice can be joined with compassion, how that may open a more spacious and energized heart, and why receiving love can be difficult for many practitioners.
short_summary: A close comparison of eros and the Brahmaviharas, with practical suggestions for combining erotic imaginal practice and compassion.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains why he wants to spell out the topic more fully and sets up the question of eros in relation to the Brahmaviharas.

chapters.title: Eros and imaginal love
chapters.description: He defines eros in the imaginal realm as a form of love with attraction, beauty, and contact, and distinguishes it from simple imaginative support in mettā practice.

chapters.title: Shared ground with the Brahmaviharas
chapters.description: The talk covers important overlaps, including care, equanimity, and love directed toward phenomena as well as beings.

chapters.title: How eros differs
chapters.description: He clarifies that eros includes desire, delight, beauty, and a love for uniqueness, while mettā remains unconditional and not based on attraction.

chapters.title: Particularity and the modern self
chapters.description: Burbea explores personal love for particulars, the modern sense of self, and why uniqueness may need to be loved rather than erased.

chapters.title: Receiving love
chapters.description: He describes the difficulty of receiving love, ambivalence about being seen, and the role of trust, pacing, and imaginal figures in opening to a loving gaze.

chapters.title: Less and more fabrication
chapters.description: The discussion turns to how the Brahmaviharas simplify self and other, while eros complicates and enriches them, often bringing cosmic ease and expanded compassion.

chapters.title: Combining practice
chapters.description: Burbea suggests that eros, including sexual energy, can be brought into mettā and compassion practice in creative ways that add energy and range rather than distraction.

chapters.title: Eros as fuel
chapters.description: He closes by showing how erotic imaginal practice can support compassion, bliss, samādhi, and equanimity, while widening the sense of sacredness that practice can disclose.

title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a wide-ranging exploration of how body, sexuality, and self are shaped by what he calls the soulmaking dynamic. He first pauses to define “shadow” as the blind spots created by any act of insight, then turns to the risk of reducing Buddhism to simplism and non-engagement. From there he develops a richer account of eros, imaginal perception, gender, and the body as something that can keep opening into new modes of knowing.
short_summary: A talk on shadow, simplism, and the way soulmaking opens body, sexuality, and perception into greater complexity.

chapters.title: Shadow of Insight
chapters.description: Burbea defines shadow as the blind spot that any insight or perspective inevitably casts.

chapters.title: Sexuality and Complexity
chapters.description: He returns to the earlier discussion of sexuality as a historical and psychic deepening of selfhood, drawing on Foucault.

chapters.title: Against Simplism
chapters.description: He criticizes a lazy form of Dharma that uses no-self and renunciation to avoid engagement with complexity.

chapters.title: Gender and Opening
chapters.description: He suggests that cultural shifts around gender and sexual orientation can be understood as soulmaking movements.

chapters.title: Body as Expressive Field
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the body, energy body, movement, and the ways imaginal practice can widen perception.

chapters.title: Erotic Perception
chapters.description: Burbea explores how eros can alter the way the body appears, using examples from imagination, beauty, and lived context.

chapters.title: Freud and Polymorphous Eros
chapters.description: He contrasts soulmaking with Freud’s account of infant sexuality and the contraction of erotic possibility through training.

chapters.title: Infinite Modes of Knowing
chapters.description: The body is described as endlessly revealing, with more possible ways of sensing, knowing, and conceiving than modern reductionism allows.

chapters.title: Body, Soul, and View
chapters.description: He ends by treating the body as a soulscape and a locus of epistemic potential, asking what soulmaking wants to open next.

title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores sexuality through the lens of dependent origination, asking how views, assumptions, and cultural inheritances shape what sex seems to be. He then moves into a wider imaginal territory, using a long reading from the myth of Inanna and Dumuzi to suggest a more reverent, cosmic, and soulmaking understanding of eros. The talk closes by examining modern identity and the ways sexuality can become over-defined, while returning to a quiet question: what actually opens experience, and what contracts it?
short_summary: A careful inquiry into sexuality, from emptiness and cultural conditioning to mythic, imaginal, and soulmaking perspectives.

chapters.title: Sex as a view
chapters.description: The talk begins by framing sexuality as empty and dependently arisen, not a fixed thing independent of how it is seen.

chapters.title: Reduction and reaction
chapters.description: Rob gives examples of reductionist and dismissive views of love and sex, including scientific, moral, and Dharma contexts.

chapters.title: Inherited moral frames
chapters.description: He reflects on Christian and Buddhist moral inheritances and how they shape what people judge, hide, or condemn around sexuality.

chapters.title: Questioning the binaries
chapters.description: He challenges the idea that sex is simply a distraction or that one must choose between sensuality and the unfabricated.

chapters.title: Inanna and Dumuzi
chapters.description: A long mythic passage presents lovemaking as sacred, fertile, and participatory in something larger than the private self.

chapters.title: Cosmic sexuality
chapters.description: He interprets the Inanna material as a vision of sexuality tied to divinity, abundance, and the fertility of the world.

chapters.title: Sex and soulmaking
chapters.description: The talk turns to intentions, ego, pleasure, stress release, and the possibility that sex can become soulmaking through imaginal openness.

chapters.title: Foucault and identity
chapters.description: Rob uses Foucault to trace how modern culture made sexuality more central to identity, diagnosis, and self-scrutiny.

chapters.title: Kindness in inquiry
chapters.description: He ends by returning to the practical test of whether a way of relating is soulmaking and emphasizes kindness over rigidity.

title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how our views of body, sensuality, sexuality, and beauty are shaped by hidden assumptions about moderation, health, and what counts as a legitimate spiritual path. He contrasts modern habits of flattening experience with a more open sense of eros, soulmaking, and the imaginal, where sensuality can be understood as dimensional rather than merely pleasant. The talk moves from religious longing and monastic renunciation into questions of art, nature, and ecology, asking what becomes possible when beauty is not reduced to a simple material or psychological function.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into eros, beauty, and the way spiritual assumptions can flatten or open our relationship with the senses.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: He introduces the talk as a way of widening questions about body, sensuality, and sexuality rather than settling them.

chapters.title: Intensity and pathologizing
chapters.description: He argues that strong eros, whether spiritual, artistic, or sexual, is often misread as extreme or unhealthy.

chapters.title: Merton and total commitment
chapters.description: Thomas Merton's longing to give himself to God is used to show how devotion can look excessive from the outside.

chapters.title: Moderation and its limits
chapters.description: He questions the cultural elevation of balance and psychological health when it becomes a way of shrinking soulmaking.

chapters.title: Eros and the senses
chapters.description: He begins to connect eros with body, sensuality, and sexuality as fields of potentially endless opening.

chapters.title: Three views of practice
chapters.description: He lays out three ways of relating to sense experience: bare attention, transcendence beyond the senses, and a soulmaking or imaginal approach.

chapters.title: Beauty, depth, and belonging
chapters.description: He develops beauty as more than pleasantness, tying it to depth, intimacy, and the feeling of belonging to something larger.

chapters.title: Practice styles and flatland
chapters.description: He contrasts Mahasi-style insight and non-doing approaches with a view that preserves mystery, otherness, and erotic possibility.

chapters.title: Fabrication and sacredness
chapters.description: He closes by linking beauty to fabrication, poetry, nature, and ecology, arguing that recognizing illusion need not diminish the sacred.

title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how eros, imagination, and ritual can be read as central to spiritual life rather than peripheral or suspect. Moving through Buddhist tantra, Christian Sophia devotion, Gnostic and Hermetic texts, and Kabbalistic practice, he asks whether these traditions point toward a re-sanctified world in which perception itself becomes participatory and soulmaking. The talk keeps returning to the tension between literalism and a more dimensional, symbolic, and cosmopoetic way of looking. It closes by holding together transcendence and the sacredness of the world, with human longing as a thirst that the limited world alone cannot satisfy.
short_summary: A wide-ranging exploration of eros, tantra, and mystical ways of seeing that reframe practice, sexuality, and the world as participatory and sacred.

chapters.title: Eros at the Center
chapters.description: He opens by naming eros as central to tantric symbolism, re-enchantment, and the relationship with sense experience and awakening.

chapters.title: Origins or Telos
chapters.description: Using Nietzsche and historical examples, he contrasts the myth of origins with a teleological way of reading religious development.

chapters.title: Tradition as Growth
chapters.description: Michelangelo and Buddhist history are used to suggest that traditions may be moving toward something fuller rather than decaying from an original purity.

chapters.title: Sophia and Imaginal Prayer
chapters.description: Gichtel and Thomas Merton illustrate a Christian line in which feminine divine presence permeates the world and devotion becomes imaginal participation.

chapters.title: Ambiguous Sacred Sex
chapters.description: Gnostic and tantric materials are read as deliberately multivalent, raising the question of whether sexual imagery is symbolic, literal, or a doorway into practice.

chapters.title: Three Tantric Readings
chapters.description: He lays out symbolic, instrumental, and re-sanctifying readings of tantra, then argues that all three are active in a soulmaking approach.

chapters.title: Sun, Moon, and Cosmos
chapters.description: An imaginal episode with a woman, a heart-sun, and later a moon image becomes a way of describing erotic polarity as cosmic harmony rather than personal psychology.

chapters.title: Sex as Divine Mystery
chapters.description: With the Hermetic Asclepius and related material, he argues that sexual union can reveal and participate in divine and cosmic dimensions when seen reverently.

chapters.title: Ritual, World, and Thirst
chapters.description: Kabbalah and the Zohar are used to show ritual as world-making, and the talk ends by holding together the sacred world and the pull beyond it into the transcendent.

title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues weaving together eros, emptiness, and imaginal practice, using tantric and Kabbalistic parallels to show how desire can either fabricate a constricted world or open into sacred, non-realist participation. He revisits the insight tradition as an evolving and selective inheritance, then reads a tantric verse about passion and Vajrasattva as a compact teaching on dependent origination, divine appearance, and the transformation of perception. The talk then turns to a long reflective example from imaginal practice, before broadening into Nietzsche, Vajrayana iconography, and mystical traditions that hold transcendence and immanence together.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on eros, emptiness, and imaginal practice, with tantric, Kabbalistic, and Nietzschean parallels.

chapters.title: Weaving the threads
chapters.description: He opens by framing the talk as a continued weaving of themes already introduced in the retreat.

chapters.title: Insight as an evolving tradition
chapters.description: He describes the insight meditation tradition as selective, modern, and still developing, with influences from Zen, Dzogchen, and Mahamudra.

chapters.title: Tantric verse and Vajrasattva
chapters.description: He introduces a tantric couplet about passion and reads it as a bridge between world-arising, emptiness, and the diamond being.

chapters.title: Desire and world-making
chapters.description: Using Jung and the Buddha's language of bhava-tanha, he distinguishes craving from eros and tracks how desire fabricates worlds at different levels.

chapters.title: World rejection and transcendence
chapters.description: He places Buddhism within a broader religious pattern of world rejection, then contrasts that with a more tantric or imaginal affirmation of the world.

chapters.title: A living imaginal sequence
chapters.description: He shares a detailed personal sequence of a burnt man and a healing goddess that develops into a highly erotic, devotional image of sacred commitment.

chapters.title: Inexhaustible eros
chapters.description: He contrasts tanha with the holiness of eros, then develops the idea that erotic energy can widen divinity, re-enchant the world, and work across many kinds of objects.

chapters.title: Nietzsche and the senses
chapters.description: He reads Nietzsche on loving the world and the senses, using the passage to support a transfiguring, non-puritanical spirituality.

chapters.title: Buddha mind and Kabbalah
chapters.description: He compares Buddhist, tantric, and Kabbalistic accounts of transcendence and immanence, including the erotic union of wisdom and appearance.

title: In Love with the Way: Images of Path and of Self
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how practitioners image the path, awakening, and themselves in relation to tradition. He contrasts the usual medical-patient and religious fantasies of practice with the scientific researcher and artist fantasies, then opens the question of what is most authentic, fertile, and soulmaking. The talk moves toward a gentler inquiry: noticing where fantasy is alive, where it collapses into ego-measurement, and how different areas of practice may call for different ways of engaging.
short_summary: Rob Burbea examines four ways of imagining the Dharma path and invites listeners to notice which images are alive, constricted, or genuinely soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains why he wants to slow down and revisit a point about Eros for the path rather than rush past it.

chapters.title: Eros and path fantasy
chapters.description: He introduces the question of being in love with practice and suggests that the path is always carried by images, desire, and a sense of more to discover.

chapters.title: Medical model of practice
chapters.description: The Four Noble Truths are read through a medical diagnosis-and-cure model, showing how Dharma can be imagined as treatment for suffering.

chapters.title: Bones need flesh
chapters.description: Burbea argues that the Four Noble Truths are only a skeleton unless they are fleshed out by psyche, logos, image, and fantasy.

chapters.title: Researcher fantasy
chapters.description: Using science and figures like Galileo, Newton, Einstein, and Planck, he presents a path-image grounded in revision, openness, and new discoveries.

chapters.title: Artist fantasy
chapters.description: He turns to the path as art, beginning with creativity and then widening into a sense of participation in divine or Buddha-nature creativity.

chapters.title: Permission and scope
chapters.description: The talk explores how these fantasies overlap, how much permission we give them, and how they can expand or contract depending on the situation.

chapters.title: Five areas of practice
chapters.description: Burbea applies the inquiry to emptiness, imaginal practice, samadhi, metta, and ethics, asking what each area most truly calls forth.

chapters.title: Open-ended path
chapters.description: He closes by inviting listeners to test these fantasies, notice what is authentic or fertile, and consider whether the path itself may be open-ended.

title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores eros for the path: what it means to be genuinely drawn to practice, and how that desire shapes the way awakening is imagined. He contrasts a medical model of Dharma with scientistic, religious, and artistic models, then turns toward soulmaking as a more open-ended and personally fertile way to understand practice. The talk gradually widens from critique of attainment to a fuller account of beauty, mystery, and the movement between transcendence and immanence.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on eros, soulmaking, and open-ended practice, contrasting reduction-of-suffering models with a path shaped by beauty, imagination, and depth.

chapters.title: Eros and the path
chapters.description: The talk opens by linking eros, awakening, and the images that shape how practice is lived.

chapters.title: The medical model
chapters.description: He frames the Four Noble Truths as a reduction-of-suffering model and names its strengths and limits.

chapters.title: Tradition and replication
chapters.description: He examines a religious fantasy of practice that tries to reproduce the Buddha and place authority in the past.

chapters.title: The artistic possibility
chapters.description: An artistic model is introduced as more open-ended, allowing originality and even a kind of healthy obsession.

chapters.title: Open-ended practice
chapters.description: He contrasts attainment-oriented ideas with a practice understood as exploratory, authentic, and not confined to measurement.

chapters.title: What the soul wants
chapters.description: The talk shifts toward beauty, desire, and the question of whether practice is truly fertile for the soul.

chapters.title: Outgrowing fixed images
chapters.description: Through examples, he shows how even authentic visions of practice can become too small and need to expand.

chapters.title: Immanence and transcendence
chapters.description: He asks what allows practice to become soulmaking, and how transcendence can return to the world as depth and holiness.

chapters.title: Icons, not idols
chapters.description: The closing movement argues for seeing path-images as icons—open, inexhaustible, and capable of keeping eros alive without collapsing into craving.

title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring eros in relation to the path, asking what kinds of awakening can actually support desire, imagination, and soulmaking. He contrasts deflationary models of practice with more expansive ones, moving through papancha, emptiness, Vajrayana imagery, and the way different views shape what feels possible on the path. The talk gradually widens from a Buddhist analysis of sense experience into a broader inquiry into psychology, culture, and the limits people place on eros.
short_summary: Rob Burbea examines how eros, imagination, and views of awakening shape the path, and how more expansive frameworks can open soulmaking rather than reduce practice to restraint or bare process.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: He frames the talk around eros, the path, and how they already include questions of awakening and the world.

chapters.title: Pleasure and reduction
chapters.description: He contrasts common teachings about sense pleasure and papancha with more nuanced ways of understanding what the mind is doing.

chapters.title: Papancha reconsidered
chapters.description: He revisits papancha as objectification and fabrication, then distinguishes different levels at which experience can be simplified or emptied.

chapters.title: Cooking and devotion
chapters.description: Using the Gaia House kitchen, he shows how ordinary activities can carry fantasy, beauty, and soulmaking beyond simple care.

chapters.title: Vajrayana and emptiness
chapters.description: He turns to Vajrayana mandala symbolism and the Heart Sutra to show different ways appearance, wisdom, and emptiness can be related.

chapters.title: Eros in the path
chapters.description: He develops the idea that the degree and kind of eros shape the kind of path a person can inhabit, with examples from lovers, food, and embodied practice.

chapters.title: History and deflation
chapters.description: He draws on Richard Tarnas, Nietzsche, William James, and Richard Rorty to show how sobriety, pragmatism, and deflation can narrow the available imagination.

chapters.title: Fear and limitation
chapters.description: With Douglas Fairbairn, he explores how fear of disappointment can create an anti-libidinal stance that dampens eros and soulmaking.

chapters.title: A larger path
chapters.description: He closes by arguing that more eros requires a larger vision of awakening, one that can keep opening into depth, divinity, and multidimensionality.

title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the early Buddhist path as having a clear transcendent thrust: not just calmer experience, but a movement beyond sense experience, fabrication, and the world. He tracks this through repeated Pali Canon passages, then asks what it means for eros, soul-making, and the meaning of the “holy life.” The talk closes by setting early Buddhism alongside later Mahayana and modern attitudes, asking how each orients us toward transcendence, immanence, and the world.
short_summary: A close reading of early Buddhist texts on the unfabricated, and a reflection on how eros may be directed toward transcendence, the holy life, and the world.

chapters.title: Eros and the path
chapters.description: Burbea opens by asking where eros is directed in practice and whether the path itself can be understood as a form of soul-making.

chapters.title: Beyond the senses
chapters.description: He reads passages from the Samyutta Nikaya that describe liberation as a dimension where the sense bases cease and dukkha ends.

chapters.title: Beyond ordinary perception
chapters.description: A Majjhima Nikaya passage is used to show that even refined jhana states are still not the final goal, which lies beyond the whole field of perception.

chapters.title: The unfabricated
chapters.description: He turns to the Udana and a strong instruction to 'smash' the aggregates, interpreting it as meditative unfabrication rather than literal destruction.

chapters.title: Consciousness without attribute
chapters.description: Burbea gathers more Pali passages that describe an objectless, attribute-free mode beyond the six senses, beyond the immaterial realms, and beyond concentration alone.

chapters.title: No coming, no going
chapters.description: Another cluster of verses points to a dimension without arising or passing away, which he presents as utterly beyond time, process, and objecthood.

chapters.title: Via negativa and longing
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion into apophatic language and then brings in Gregory of Nyssa to suggest that human longing may point beyond the limited world.

chapters.title: The soul's more
chapters.description: Returning to eros and soul-making, he distinguishes ordinary accumulation from a deeper 'more' that seeks the transcendent and maps onto jhana and nibbana.

chapters.title: The holy life
chapters.description: Burbea explains how the phrase 'the holy life' functions in the Pali Canon, then contrasts the early Buddhist renunciate aim with the Mahayana Bodhisattva ideal.

title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a close examination of doubt, asking how it can become a workable instrument of discernment rather than a source of paralysis. He contrasts eros and craving, then extends the same kind of discrimination to rest, metta, imaginal practice, and jhana, showing how each can be present in ways that are either soulmaking or merely comforting. The talk keeps returning to emptiness and dependent origination as a basis for letting images, longing, and practice be taken seriously without being reified.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of how to discern eros from craving, and how emptiness, longing, rest, and jhana fit into soulmaking practice.

chapters.title: Practice as preparation
chapters.description: He opens by naming the skills that make this investigation possible: mindfulness, energy body work, emotion, and insight into emptiness.

chapters.title: Eros or craving
chapters.description: He develops the central distinction between eros and craving, emphasizing how eros opens the energy body and craving contracts it.

chapters.title: Wise effort
chapters.description: Using the Buddha’s four bases of success and examples from practice, he shows how desire and effort can be handled without either strain or passivity.

chapters.title: Questioning and experiment
chapters.description: He encourages daring, careful experimentation, and notes that eros and craving can become tangled without that meaning the whole practice should be discarded.

chapters.title: Imaginal autonomy
chapters.description: He turns to Piaget, then offers a story of an imaginal figure acting with its own intelligence, to show why the imaginal is not just wish fulfillment.

chapters.title: Not realism, but relation
chapters.description: With James Hillman and many examples, he argues that the main danger is identification, realism, and objectification, not the mere presence of strong images.

chapters.title: Emptiness as basis
chapters.description: He places emptiness at the foundation of the path and reframes the issue as one of efficacy and potency rather than reality claims.

chapters.title: Rest and jhana
chapters.description: He distinguishes shutting down from resting in living relationship, then compares imaginal aliveness with states of less fabrication and jhana.

chapters.title: Longing and devotion
chapters.description: He closes by asking where the imaginal is alive in our lives, and by treating longing as a clue to both personal desire and what we are called to serve.

title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores doubt around erotic imaginal practice by asking whether it is escapism, wish fulfillment, or a deeper way of meeting life. He turns those questions into direct inquiry, distinguishes eros from craving, and traces how imaginal practice can change engagement, perception, and the sense of the world. The talk moves from conceptual discernment to personal examples, then toward a wider view of the imaginal as relational, creative, and alive.
short_summary: A careful inquiry into doubt, eros, and imaginal practice, contrasting craving and escape with a more alive and discerning way of relating to image and world.

chapters.title: Opening the doubt
chapters.description: He names the normal doubt that arises around erotic imaginal practice and proposes turning uncertainty into inquiry rather than paralysis.

chapters.title: Image asks for translation
chapters.description: He returns to earlier work on the imaginal and explains how images often carry a sense of duty and ask to be translated into life.

chapters.title: Non-engagement or depth
chapters.description: He questions the image of renunciation and non-engagement, asking whether imaginal practice really withdraws from life or deepens contact with it.

chapters.title: Fleeing reality revisited
chapters.description: He reframes the accusation of fleeing reality by asking what is meant by reality and whether dismissing the imaginal may itself be a kind of avoidance.

chapters.title: Eros versus craving
chapters.description: He introduces a sharper discernment between eros and craving, showing how desire can either narrow the heart or open and energize the whole being.

chapters.title: Fire, risk, and unbinding
chapters.description: He explores how eros is like fire: useful and dangerous, linked to Buddhist fire imagery, the Buddha’s reversal of it, and the meaning of Nibbana as unbinding.

chapters.title: Doubt, suffering, and path
chapters.description: He places the work in a wider mythic and practical frame, using Dionysus, Vajrayana, Mahasi-style retreat hardship, and Ajahn Chah’s tears to ask what kinds of risk and suffering a path can hold.

chapters.title: Personal movement in practice
chapters.description: He shares his own gradual movement from caution toward trust in erotic imaginal work, including dreams, ambivalence, and the slow opening of relationship with image and nature.

chapters.title: Living relationship and infinity
chapters.description: He ends by distinguishing imaginal practice from ordinary fantasy through sensitivity, logos, intentionality, and the theater of image, then contrasts a finite path of oneness with the infinite creative possibilities of eros and imaginal relating.

title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea uses the analogy of musical hearing to ask whether the more imaginal dimensions of experience are “real,” or whether a better question is whether they are important and soul-making. From there he critiques flat cultural assumptions about science, realism, and mindfulness, and builds a case for a participatory view in which perception, mind, and world are not separate in the way modernity often assumes. The talk then widens into a careful sketch of soulmaking, Eros, and Logos, including implications for psychology, pathologies, developmental theory, and the limits of any conceptual framework.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on imaginal perception, participation, and soulmaking, framed against modern realism and developmental psychology.

chapters.title: Music and limited hearing
chapters.description: Burbea opens with a musical anecdote to show how a very limited mode of perception can miss most of what is actually there.

chapters.title: Real or soul-making
chapters.description: He develops the music analogy into a larger question of whether an experience is real, important, or soul-making, and extends it to nature, other people, and the cosmos.

chapters.title: Culture and realism
chapters.description: The talk turns to how dominant cultural views gain authority, how clashes of realism arise, and why scientific method should not be confused with a complete account of reality.

chapters.title: Frameworks that support
chapters.description: Burbea argues for a stable conceptual framework that can withstand cultural pressure while opening experience, rather than shutting it down through inherited assumptions.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and participation
chapters.description: He begins to articulate soulmaking more directly, including the role of Eros, the non-separation of citta and reality, and the idea of participation as a fertile way of understanding perception and imagination.

chapters.title: Valid because valuable
chapters.description: The argument deepens around participation: an idea can matter because it enlarges experience, and its value is tied to its capacity to stimulate soulmaking rather than to a claim of final metaphysical proof.

chapters.title: Eros and development
chapters.description: Burbea re-reads developmental psychology through the lens of soulmaking, contrasting his view with Freud, Fairbairn, and Kohut, and suggesting that the soul wants expansion across self, other, world, body, and mind.

chapters.title: Self, image, and pathology
chapters.description: He questions culturally inherited ideas of a fixed self and explores how blocked or one-sided imaginal development may relate to pathology, while noting that the psyche may naturally learn to see image as image.

chapters.title: Cracks in theory
chapters.description: The closing movement returns to the limits of any framework, the inevitability of cracks and expansion, and the idea that theory is a kind of theatre serving soulmaking rather than final truth.

title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how any Dharma framework also shapes what we can notice, value, and become. He argues that a useful logos must support soulmaking: imagination, eros, freedom, beauty, and the capacity for frameworks to break open rather than harden into dogma. In the second half, he turns to interpretation itself, questioning claims of neutral access to the Pali Canon and tracing how modern, scientific, and secular assumptions can quietly narrow Dharma and erase other ways of knowing.
short_summary: A talk on how conceptual frameworks shape practice, interpretation, and the possibilities of soulmaking.

chapters.title: What a logos must hold
chapters.description: He returns to the problem of how to build a conceptual framework that can make room for soulmaking, dependent origination, emptiness, and multiple ways of knowing.

chapters.title: Eros and interpretation
chapters.description: He shows how ideas about desire and eros directly change experience, and why some views support soulmaking while others constrain it.

chapters.title: When Dharma narrows
chapters.description: He warns that a narrow Dharma can become entrenched dogma, quietly limiting freedom, creativity, beauty, and the range of experience.

chapters.title: Whose Pali Canon?
chapters.description: He asks who is really being heard when people appeal to the Pali Canon, and argues that every reading comes with assumptions, agendas, and a fantasy of the Buddha.

chapters.title: Scholarship and tradition
chapters.description: He questions the claim that traditional language guarantees authenticity, and suggests that scholarship and interpretation are always creative and historically situated.

chapters.title: Origins and dialogue
chapters.description: He looks at how Mahayana and Vajrayana traditions claim continuity with the Buddha, then reframes the relationship to tradition as dialogue rather than fixed authority.

chapters.title: Epistemicide in Dharma
chapters.description: He introduces epistemicide to name the erasure of embodied, imaginal, and culturally diverse ways of knowing, including within some secular Dharma settings.

chapters.title: Mindfulness is not enough
chapters.description: He argues that stripped-down secular Dharma can function like a missionary worldview unless it also opens deeper insight, dependent origination, and liberating ways of looking.

chapters.title: Training ways of looking
chapters.description: He closes by using contemporary philosophy to show that epistemology is always lived, then returns to meditation as the training of flexible states of mind and ways of looking.

title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the role of logos, or conceptual framework, in Dharma practice: not as abstract theory, but as something that shapes navigation, balance, and the very texture of experience. He moves from trellis and tree metaphors to emptiness, fabrication, and ways of looking, then widens into soulmaking, embodiment, relationship, death, ecology, divinity, and the limits of modernist views. The talk ends by asking for a framework that can include plural ways of knowing without flattening them into a single dogma.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on logos as a living conceptual framework that supports practice, opens experience, and must make room for soulmaking, emptiness, and plural ways of knowing.

chapters.title: Logos as support
chapters.description: He argues that every path needs a conceptual framework to hold practices together, guide navigation, and provide stability.

chapters.title: Trellis and tree
chapters.description: Using the images of a trellis and then a fruit tree, he shows how logos can be organic, integrated, responsive, and breakable.

chapters.title: Concept beyond thought
chapters.description: He clarifies that conceptuality is not just discursive thinking and is still operating even in refined meditative absorption.

chapters.title: Fabrication and emptiness
chapters.description: The talk turns to the spectrum of fabrication, dependent origination, emptiness, and the legitimacy of multiple ways of looking.

chapters.title: What the frame must hold
chapters.description: He broadens the needed framework to include imaginal life, eros, body, relationships, death, the earth, beauty, psychology, sacredness, and divinity.

chapters.title: History and conditioning
chapters.description: Concept and experience are described as mutually conditioning, and the framework is placed within history and society rather than treated as ahistorical truth.

chapters.title: Modern self and soulmaking
chapters.description: A retreat story, the construction of the modernist self, and the claim that fabrication can still be genuinely soulmaking are brought together.

chapters.title: Holy bodies and Christ
chapters.description: He uses the image of holding a loved one's feet, then turns to Christ and Paul to show how devotion can generate theology and a larger cosmology.

chapters.title: Plural knowing
chapters.description: The final movement gathers the need for a fertile, non-dogmatic framework that avoids epistemicide, includes dependent origination and emptiness, and leaves room for a broad range of embodied ways of knowing.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a talk on balance in imaginal practice, focusing on how attention can become lopsided and how to restore fertility without suppressing experience. He distinguishes intensity from potency, then shows how sexual energy, erotic image, and the energy body can be worked with more skillfully. The talk closes by widening the map: shifts among desire, image, body, heart, head, and conceptual framework, and how a practice can be deliberately steered.
short_summary: A practical talk on balancing attention in imaginal practice, with attention to sexual energy, erotic tension, and the role of conceptual framework.

chapters.title: Balance in Practice
chapters.description: He frames the talk around balance, sustainability, and fertility in imaginal practice, using attention as the main lever.

chapters.title: Heart and Image
chapters.description: A case example shows how attention can overconcentrate in the heart and energy body, crowding out the imaginal dimension.

chapters.title: Intensity and Potency
chapters.description: He names the difference between something feeling intense and something actually being transformative or soulmaking.

chapters.title: Sexual Energy
chapters.description: The talk turns to sexual arousal, suggesting a wider, whole-body attention that can let energy spread and transform into bliss.

chapters.title: Union or Participation
chapters.description: He describes two possible directions in erotic imaginal practice: dissolving into oneness, or sustaining two-ness and participation.

chapters.title: Shifting the Emphasis
chapters.description: He generalizes the balancing move across desire, image, energy body, emotional tone, and the three centers of belly, heart, and head.

chapters.title: Degrees of Fabrication
chapters.description: He revisits the distinction between bare attention, a middle mode, and the deliberate fabrication of imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Framework as Vessel
chapters.description: The closing section emphasizes conceptual framework as a container that offers orientation, trust, stability, and guidance.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how desire can be worked with as part of imaginal practice rather than simply being suppressed or rejected. He moves from the question of balance into a series of examples: an unreachable beloved, a retreatant’s forest experience, and several ways of re-perceiving longing so that self, other, and world gain depth. The talk ends by contrasting an anatta reading of desire with ways of seeing eros as sacred, alive, and already participating in a larger divine or imaginal field.
short_summary: A talk on balancing eros and imaginal practice so desire can open into depth, peace, and soulmaking rather than contraction.

chapters.title: Eros and balance
chapters.description: Burbea frames the talk as a question of how to work with eros so it becomes fertile and soulmaking rather than merely alternating between fire and steadiness.

chapters.title: The unreachable beloved
chapters.description: He offers a relational example in which painful attraction can be entered imaginally, enlarging the other beyond a flat, concrete reality.

chapters.title: Beyond 'let go'
chapters.description: He questions the simplicity of the usual advice to let go and suggests that, in complex situations, opening to the image may be more fruitful.

chapters.title: A transforming story
chapters.description: A long student example shows frustration, inferiority, and longing opening into vivid animal-erotic imagery, connection, and holiness.

chapters.title: Include the self
chapters.description: He argues that balance in the soul-making dynamic requires seeing not only the beloved other but also the beauty and eros of the self.

chapters.title: Forest and benefactors
chapters.description: A retreatant’s account moves from grief with trees and cold air into specific imaginal love from several benefactors, showing pain opening into care.

chapters.title: Love in the imaginal
chapters.description: Burbea draws out the lesson that imaginal perception already contains love and that love can flow both from the image and back toward the practitioner.

chapters.title: A balanced constellation
chapters.description: He broadens the teaching to include object, self, world, and eros, warning against reification and showing how multidimensionality loosens fixation.

chapters.title: Desire, divinity, and time
chapters.description: Using Blake and Henri Corbin, he explores desire as divine influx, desire as already happening in sacred time, and then contrasts this with an anatta reading that makes desire fade.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues his discussion of mastery in practice by treating it as a fluid art of navigation rather than a fixed ideal. He moves through several ways of working with desire and craving: letting go, noticing vedanā and pressure, meeting underlying emotion with kindness, and skillfully redirecting toward nourishment or spaciousness. In the second half he turns to imaginal practice, looking at how to stay with erotic images, how to meet what feels hard to bear, and how shifting emphasis can open the practice without losing its tension.
short_summary: A talk on mastery as flexible response, then a close look at working skillfully with desire, craving, and erotic imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Mastery as navigation
chapters.description: He frames mastery as fluid balance: moment-to-moment responding, not rigidly repeating one method.

chapters.title: Why range matters
chapters.description: He contrasts habitual one-sided practice with the wider range needed for freedom and insight.

chapters.title: Letting desire pass
chapters.description: He explores noticing pleasure, seeing where it goes, and using impermanence to support letting go.

chapters.title: Vedanā and craving
chapters.description: He shifts to pleasant and unpleasant feeling tones, tracing craving and the pressure that can be tolerated and allowed.

chapters.title: Kindness for emotion
chapters.description: He introduces a gentler response: caring for the self and holding the underlying emotion with mindfulness and metta.

chapters.title: Subtle skill in practice
chapters.description: He emphasizes delicate shifts of attention and the artistry of micro-adjustments in the heat of practice.

chapters.title: Skillful redirection
chapters.description: He presents conscious turning away from desire toward nourishment, samadhi, metta, spaciousness, and less fabrication.

chapters.title: Opening desire's depth
chapters.description: Drawing on an earlier talk, he outlines a three-step inquiry into what is really wanted and how to open to desire's energy.

chapters.title: Working with eros
chapters.description: He focuses on staying with imaginal images, meeting what is hard to bear, trusting dissolution, and distinguishing eros from equanimity.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this second part of the talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring how to build a vessel that can hold eros and imaginal practice without being overwhelmed by them. He emphasizes samadhi as nourishment and balance rather than just concentration, then broadens the frame to include rootedness, spaciousness, strength, and inquiry as supports for practice. The talk closes by turning to mandalas and paired images such as Akshobhya, Amitabha, Ratnasambhava, and Amoghasiddhi as ways of bringing emptiness, feeling, and action into a living balance.
short_summary: A talk on samadhi, equanimity, and imaginal balance as supports for working skillfully with eros.

chapters.title: Picking up the thread
chapters.description: Burbea recaps the earlier material on ethics, trust, equanimity, and skill with emotions and energies before continuing the list of supports for this work.

chapters.title: Samadhi beyond focus
chapters.description: He redefines samadhi as well-being and less fabrication, not simply fixed attention.

chapters.title: Nourishment and balance
chapters.description: The talk explores how meditative nourishment reduces dependence on sense pleasure and ego reassurance, making the practitioner less easily thrown off balance.

chapters.title: Why understanding matters
chapters.description: Burbea argues that understanding how practice works changes what we choose in meditation and how we regard thoughts, images, and emotions.

chapters.title: Rootedness and earth
chapters.description: He turns to hara practice, earth connection, and the Buddha touching the earth as examples of imaginal rootedness and strength.

chapters.title: Spacious equanimity
chapters.description: The talk contrasts solidity with spacious, insubstantial forms of equanimity that allow things to come and go without entanglement.

chapters.title: Strength, libido, vitality
chapters.description: Burbea discusses images of strength, the role of libido and vitality, and the need to avoid pathologizing a person's natural tendencies.

chapters.title: Dynamic balance
chapters.description: He frames balance as fluid and responsive, then uses the jhanas to show that qualities of stillness and equanimity can be accessed, revisited, and trusted over time.

chapters.title: Range and inquiry
chapters.description: The talk widens into a range of being that includes arousal, stillness, mindfulness, and investigation, while questioning fixed ideals of balance.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores two alchemical sayings as a way of thinking about eros, practice, and the conditions that support a fuller path. He returns again and again to the need for a vessel that is strong and flexible enough to hold intensity, and to the question of how to work with fire without either repressing it or being overwhelmed by it. The talk then widens into preliminaries for eros practice: ethics, emotional and energetic skill, trust, and a more nuanced relation to equanimity, freedom, and duty.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of eros, vessel, and equanimity, with practical attention to ethics, energetics, and the supports needed for deeper practice.

chapters.title: Two alchemical maxims
chapters.description: Burbea introduces two alchemical sayings and frames them as broadly relevant to practice, psychotherapy, and healing.

chapters.title: Eros needs a vessel
chapters.description: He explains why opening to eros requires care, boundaries, and a vessel strong enough to hold the work without acting it out.

chapters.title: Elasticity and shattering
chapters.description: The vessel must be able to stretch, and sometimes break, as development outgrows old forms and structures.

chapters.title: Fire and its dangers
chapters.description: He turns to fire as effort, intensity, and eros itself, stressing both the need for mastery and the inevitability of risk and change.

chapters.title: Dionysus and dismemberment
chapters.description: Drawing on James Hillman and Euripides, he uses Dionysian imagery to show eros as disruptive, archetypal, and not reducible to personal blame.

chapters.title: Preliminaries for practice
chapters.description: Burbea asks what must be in place before deeper eros work: ethics, preliminary disciplines, conceptual framing, sangha, and a willingness to question assumptions.

chapters.title: Ethics and rootedness
chapters.description: He argues that eros practice needs a heartfelt, embodied commitment to ethics, not just obedience to rules or fear of consequences.

chapters.title: Energetics and trust
chapters.description: He adds skill with emotion and energy, then a modest trust that allows experimentation and reveals eros as something potentially wise and meaningful.

chapters.title: Equanimity and duty
chapters.description: The talk closes by rethinking equanimity as balance and spaciousness, then linking freedom, imaginal duty, and a disciplined middle way in relation to what matters.
