chapters.title: Why simplicity fails
chapters.description: He turns to modern complexity, eros, art, and personality, suggesting that simplistic teachings can fail to nourish the richness of life.

chapters.title: Modern suffering and self
chapters.description: Burbea notes that much contemporary suffering sits at the level of personality, and asks for other ways of opening the notion of self.

chapters.title: Meeting the inner critic
chapters.description: He introduces the inner critic as a familiar structure and names loving-kindness and insight into emptiness as two ways of softening it.

chapters.title: From deconstructing to relating
chapters.description: The talk shifts to an imaginal move: treating the critic as a figure, entering dialogue, and allowing relationship to change the experience.

chapters.title: The clumsy protector
chapters.description: Burbea suggests the critic may be a protector in disguise, and tells a retreat story in which turning toward it reveals unexpected kindness.

chapters.title: Grief and the mother image
chapters.description: A second story shows how a dead mother can remain alive as an image in the psyche, and how responding to that image can open a freer and more beautiful relation.

title: Meditation: Opening to Aliveness
speaker: Tara Brach
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC)
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with settling posture, breath, and intention, then moves into a full-body scan that emphasizes softness, sensitivity, and aliveness. Tara Brach invites an embodied noticing of the senses, sound, and the background of awareness, with repeated reminders that the mind can drift and return without judgment. The practice closes by deepening into the felt earth-body and the spacious silence that holds changing experience.
short_summary: A guided meditation on opening to bodily aliveness, sound, and the awareness that can receive experience without judgment.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The meditation opens with posture, balance, and a few deliberate breaths to establish alertness and ease.

chapters.title: Intention and Aum
chapters.description: Attention moves to the heart, where she asks about sincere intention and introduces a brief chanting of Aum.

chapters.title: Softening the Face
chapters.description: The practice turns to the face, jaw, mouth, and shoulders, using relaxation to invite receptivity rather than force.

chapters.title: Heart and Belly
chapters.description: Awareness widens through the chest and belly, with the reminder that smiling into experience is a way of making space for it.

chapters.title: Pelvis and Earth Body
chapters.description: The scan continues through the pelvic region, legs, and feet, opening into the sense of the body as a living field and the earth beneath it.

chapters.title: Sound and Presence
chapters.description: Sound is folded into the practice, and the talk points to an alert stillness in the background of hearing and sensing.

chapters.title: Returning Fresh
chapters.description: She names the ordinary drift into story and offers a simple, nonjudgmental return to the senses and the breath.

chapters.title: Deepening Into Silence
chapters.description: The closing sections revisit relaxing more fully, sinking into the earth-body, and resting in the vast awake silence that remains aware.

venue: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC

title: Earth's Crisis - On the Edge of the Roof - (on climate change)
speaker: Tara Brach
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC) This talk views the ecological dis-ease of our planet through the lens of our evolutionary unfolding. We explore the egoic trance that has precipitated the destruction of our environment, and the inner practices of presence that enable us to respond from love and wisdom.
podcast_description: Tara Brach explores climate crisis through Dharma, using Rumi, the Parsifal legend, and Joanna Macy to show how denial, grasping, and disconnection keep both people and the planet in a kind of trance. She moves from the scale of ecological loss to the inner work of feeling, presence, and belonging, then toward the question of what right action looks like without certainty about outcomes. The talk closes with a prayerful invitation to listen more deeply to the living earth and respond from care.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on climate crisis, trance, grief, and the practice of responding from belonging rather than fear.

chapters.title: Opening the crisis
chapters.description: Brach frames the climate emergency as both a planetary event and a spiritual challenge, linking ecological harm to the habits of indifference and waking up.

chapters.title: Rumi and Parsifal
chapters.description: Rumi's warning about being drunk at the edge of the roof becomes a bridge into the Parsifal legend, where one honest question restores life to a wasteland.

chapters.title: Trance and consumption
chapters.description: She describes the egoic trance as grasping, numbness, and objectifying the world, drawing on the Sutra on the Son's Flesh and the logic of endless consumption.

chapters.title: Separation and entitlement
chapters.description: The talk expands into a developmental view of separation, fear, and entitlement, including cultural patterns of growth, extraction, and a sense that humans stand apart from the earth.

chapters.title: Digital distance
chapters.description: A contemporary texting story leads into a reflection on how cyber-life can flatten depth, embodiment, and compassion, even while increasing information.

chapters.title: Earth as belonging
chapters.description: Through Joanna Macy and Chief Seattle, Brach emphasizes that the earth is not a supply source but a shared body, and that shutting down in fear is a central danger.

chapters.title: Feeling and action
chapters.description: She shifts from diagnosis to practice: deepening attention, feeling what is here, not relying on hope of results, and asking what the earth requires of us now.

chapters.title: Collective awakening
chapters.description: Brach points toward systemic change, community, and teachings from Paul Hawken, Darwin, and Emerson that reframe life as interdependent, urgent, and worthy of wonder.

chapters.title: Listening and prayer
chapters.description: The closing turns into a brief contemplative appeal to hear the animals, plants, and planet itself, and to let prayer become response.

venue: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC

title: Buddhist Perspective On Climate Change
speaker: Sayadaw Vivekananda
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) Observed changes in the climate system, attribution of climate change, Buddhist perspective, Buddhist response to climate change.
podcast_description: This talk examines climate change first through the lens of observed science, then through a Buddhist ethical frame. After summarizing major findings from the IPCC and a U.S. regional assessment, Sayadaw Vivekananda turns to Nepal as a vivid example of unequal impact, before widening into questions of non-harming, compassion, greed, responsibility, and future generations. The talk ends by linking mindfulness practice to a quieter, more restrained relationship with the natural world.
short_summary: A Buddhist reading of climate change that moves from scientific evidence to ethics, non-harming, and universal responsibility.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: The talk introduces the subject and outlines a path from climate science to Buddhist responsibility.

chapters.title: What climate science shows
chapters.description: He reviews key IPCC findings on warming, oceans, ice loss, sea level rise, and greenhouse gases.

chapters.title: Human influence and local impacts
chapters.description: The talk turns to attribution, the warming since the industrial era, and examples from the northeastern United States.

chapters.title: Nepal and the Himalayas
chapters.description: He describes Nepal’s vulnerability, glacier retreat, black carbon, and downstream risks to water and food security.

chapters.title: Causes of the crisis
chapters.description: Natural causes are mentioned, but the focus settles on industrialization and human-caused greenhouse gas emissions.

chapters.title: Harmony with nature
chapters.description: Buddhist teaching is presented as cooperation rather than domination, using Peter Harvey and D.T. Suzuki as supporting voices.

chapters.title: Non-harming and metta
chapters.description: He expands the first precept, ahimsa, and the brahmaviharas to include animals, ecosystems, and all sentient beings.

chapters.title: Greed and responsibility
chapters.description: The talk connects climate change to consumer greed, the economic system, and practical ways of reducing harm and emissions.

chapters.title: Trees and future generations
chapters.description: A tree-spirit story, texts on planting groves, and a Dalai Lama quotation are used to close on gratitude and universal responsibility.

title: Practice & Climate Change
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) Knowing your refuge. Cultivating resilience. Alignment with authentic response.
podcast_description: Thanissara reflects on practice as preparation for the moments when fear, grief, and collective crisis press on the heart. Using climate change as the main example, she draws practice toward refuge, resilience, and the possibility of responding from a steadier and less separative mind. The talk moves from basic mindfulness and ethics into samadhi, renunciation, and the quiet capacity to meet uncertainty without collapsing into reactivity.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how refuge, ethics, and gathered attention can help us meet climate change and other overwhelming realities with resilience and clear response.

chapters.title: Practice as preparation
chapters.description: The talk opens by framing retreat practice as training for the moments when strong experience hits the heart and we must choose between reactivity and wise pause.

chapters.title: Climate change and activation
chapters.description: Thanissara names climate change as a major pressure on the psyche and explains how it can trigger fear, overwhelm, and old patterns of distraction.

chapters.title: A shift in consciousness
chapters.description: She argues, with Einstein and Dharma language, that problems cannot be met from the same separative mindset that helps create them.

chapters.title: Refuge in Buddha Dharma Sangha
chapters.description: The talk unfolds the three refuges as living realities: inspiration, teachings, and the capacity to practice, rather than as mere formulas.

chapters.title: Ethics and conscience
chapters.description: Thanissara turns to the five precepts, conscience, and restraint as a grounded ethical base that protects both oneself and others.

chapters.title: Courage and resilience
chapters.description: Examples from Aung San Suu Kyi and the retreat context lead into a discussion of resilience as the ability to gather, steady, and return.

chapters.title: Knowing awareness directly
chapters.description: Ajahn Tate's distinction between awareness and mental activity becomes a way to practice with breath, recognize the listening heart, and hold grief without being ruled by it.

chapters.title: Simplify and let go
chapters.description: Using Ajahn Lee's bridge analogy and the principle of nekkhamma, she explains how ethics, samadhi, and wisdom support a life that can release grasping and consume less.

chapters.title: The peace that remains
chapters.description: The closing section returns to surrender, the naked moment of awareness, and a peace that can remain present even in unstable times, from which authentic response may emerge.

title: Wise Action for the Earth
speaker: David Loy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: David Loy frames the ecological crisis through a Buddhist lens, arguing that the sense of a separate self is both the root of personal suffering and a collective source of environmental harm. He moves from dukkha and non-separation to bodhisattva practice, inner training, and forms of action that respond to climate crisis without losing balance. The talk closes with Rumi, Joanna Macy, Howard Thurman, and a brief Q&A that returns to resilience, systems change, and how language itself can hide or reveal our intimacy with the earth.
short_summary: A Dharma talk linking Buddhist teachings on self and suffering to ecological awakening, bodhisattva action, and systems change.

chapters.title: Buddhism and crisis
chapters.description: David opens by asking why the Buddha still matters for understanding the ecological crisis and proposes a parallel between personal dukkha and our collective situation.

chapters.title: Separate self, shared suffering
chapters.description: He develops the idea that the normal sense of a separate self is insecure, internally ungrounded, and tied to the feeling that something is missing.

chapters.title: Forgetting the self
chapters.description: Using Dogen and Nisargadatta, he describes awakening as moving beyond self-concern into intimacy, wisdom, and compassion.

chapters.title: From awakening to action
chapters.description: The talk turns toward bodhisattva responsibility, the earth as our larger body, and Rumi’s image of the worm waking into the vineyard.

chapters.title: A collective predicament
chapters.description: A Shantideva passage and Bill McKibben’s climate argument are used to show that the crisis is systemic and cannot be solved by individual effort alone.

chapters.title: Three kinds of transformation
chapters.description: Drawing on Joanna Macy, he outlines holding actions, building alternatives, and shifting consciousness, then closes with Howard Thurman on vocation and aliveness.

chapters.title: Questions and closing images
chapters.description: In the Q&A, he reflects on language, resilience, contagion, and a Spanish song about singing together so they cannot stop us.

title: Right Action on Climate
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: Ayya Anandabodhi speaks about climate change as an ethical and Dharma issue, beginning with the sense of Earth as sacred and moving through reasons Buddhists may feel hesitant to engage. She uses the Buddha’s care for a sick monk, Joanna Macy’s phrase about honoring pain for the world, and a series of concrete suggestions to show how grief can become steadiness and action. The talk then widens into practical organizing: speaking truthfully, joining groups, writing letters, divesting, and making personal and political commitments without losing compassion.
short_summary: A grounded Dharma talk on climate change, linking emptiness, grief, and ethical action to concrete personal and collective responses.

chapters.title: Earth as sacred
chapters.description: She opens with a felt sense of the living earth, the Welsh landscape, and the possibility of remembering sacred relationship with land.

chapters.title: Why Buddhists hesitate
chapters.description: She names the Buddhist tendency toward apathy or withdrawal around climate change and questions the idea that impermanence justifies inaction.

chapters.title: The sick monk
chapters.description: She recounts the Buddha bathing an abandoned sick monk as a model for insight joined with care and responsibility.

chapters.title: Climate as ethics
chapters.description: She parallels the monk’s abandonment with humanity’s treatment of the planet and asks whether practice can include turning toward harm rather than avoiding it.

chapters.title: Pressure and renewal
chapters.description: She places the crisis within a wider Buddhist cosmology of world cycles, then emphasizes that human choices can still speed up destruction or support renewal.

chapters.title: Resilience through practice
chapters.description: She speaks about congruence, self-respect, honoring pain for the world, and letting grief transform into strength rather than despair.

chapters.title: Concrete commitments
chapters.description: She introduces the shared action list and the Pali term adhiṭṭhāna as a firm resolve to choose a period of practice and action.

chapters.title: What to do now
chapters.description: She recommends resources, urges climate truth-telling, and gives practical examples of petitions, rallies, letters, government pressure, and organized collective action.

chapters.title: Questions and everyday choices
chapters.description: In the final exchange she discusses renewables, divestment, local banks, food choices, and the value of imperfect but sincere action guided by the larger systemic picture.

title: Today’s Climate Reality
speaker: Ayya Santussika
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: Ayya Santussika offers a plainspoken Dharma framing for climate crisis: first look directly, then understand what is happening well enough to respond. She moves through current climate evidence, likely near- and long-term impacts, and the political and economic forces that keep emissions going. The talk ends by shifting from fear toward collective action, including emissions cuts, adaptation, and the role of education, poverty reduction, and restoring natural systems.
short_summary: A grounded Dharma talk on facing climate reality clearly and responding with practical, collective action.

chapters.title: Look directly
chapters.description: The talk begins by placing climate reality in the frame of the First Noble Truth: face suffering clearly before trying to transform it.

chapters.title: What is happening now
chapters.description: She reviews present-day climate evidence from the AAAS report, including warming, ice loss, sea level rise, acidification, flooding, drought, storms, and fire.

chapters.title: Warming and wildfires
chapters.description: The talk continues with the causes of the crisis, the human role in rising CO2, and the growing scale of wildfires and megafires.

chapters.title: Impacts on life and conflict
chapters.description: Using the IPCC and a reference to Years of Living Dangerously, she describes how climate disruption affects ecosystems, health, food, water, and the risk of violence.

chapters.title: Maps of impact
chapters.description: She returns to the IPCC’s global picture and explains how water, marine systems, terrestrial systems, and food production are already being affected across continents.

chapters.title: California drought ahead
chapters.description: Michael Werner’s comments localize the talk to California, with Sierra snowpack, reservoirs, and a hotter, drier future.

chapters.title: The carbon budget
chapters.description: Drawing on Bill McKibben’s climate math, she explains the remaining carbon budget and the scale of continued fossil-fuel exploration.

chapters.title: Why action is blocked
chapters.description: She names the economic interests, misinformation, and subsidies that keep fossil fuels in place, and argues for a carbon tax and an end to new extraction.

chapters.title: What to do together
chapters.description: The closing turns to Lester Brown’s broader response—cut emissions, stabilize population growth, reduce poverty, restore ecosystems, and keep working together.

title: How To Be an Earthling
speaker: Wes Nisker
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) with Kevin Griffin on guitar
podcast_description: Wes Nisker offers a humorous Earth Day talk that moves from comic songs and playful scripture mashups into a serious reflection on ecological crisis and our place within evolution. He uses Gary Snyder, John Muir, Darwin, and his own satire to question human exceptionalism and suggest a more grounded, interdependent story. The talk closes with a simple practice of presence—here, now—paired with a call to care for the planet in ordinary, practical ways.
short_summary: A comic, serious Earth Day talk about ecology, evolution, and learning to live as part of the earth.

chapters.title: Ecological pledge
chapters.description: Wes opens with Gary Snyder’s ecological Pledge of Allegiance to frame the talk around belonging to the earth and all beings.

chapters.title: John Muir and wonder
chapters.description: He honors John Muir and reads a short poem on the earth’s continuous cycles of sunrise, sunset, and turning.

chapters.title: Crisis and comic relief
chapters.description: A song about modern anxieties turns environmental dread and media saturation into a shared, playful release.

chapters.title: Liberation and Passover
chapters.description: Wes imagines Moses, Jesus, and Buddha in contemporary terms, linking spiritual renewal with liberation from greed and bondage.

chapters.title: Earth Day and Gaia
chapters.description: He praises Earth Day as a universal holy day and invokes Gaia, Pachamama, and the planet’s gravity as forms of care and support.

chapters.title: Evolution as creation myth
chapters.description: Wes contrasts the Holocene and Anthropocene, then asks listeners to take evolution seriously as a new story of who we are.

chapters.title: Made of earth
chapters.description: He emphasizes that our bodies are literally made from earth and shared life, and urges simpler living and practical protection of the planet.

chapters.title: Enough stuff
chapters.description: Kevin Griffin’s song satirizes consumer excess and names the feeling that we already have more than enough.

chapters.title: Here and now
chapters.description: The closing teaching becomes a call-and-response on presence, reducing the message to a simple instruction: be here now.

title: Compassion As We Take Action
speaker: Jack Kornfield
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Recorded from Spirit Rock's Earth Day Gathering
podcast_description: Jack Kornfield speaks at a Spirit Rock Earth Day gathering about how compassion can carry action without being driven by guilt, anger, or exhaustion. He weaves in stories about Gary Snyder, Gabriela Mistral, Hawaiian voyaging, elephants, chimps, and Buddhist teachers to suggest a quieter kind of activism rooted in love, interconnection, and long horizons. The talk closes with a guided meditation and blessing-cord ceremony that turns intention into a lived practice of refuge, non-harming, and vow.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on activism as a practice of love, interdependence, and patient, non-attached action.

chapters.title: Opening questions
chapters.description: He invites the room to name what would help as they return from the Earth Day gathering back into ordinary life.

chapters.title: Act from love
chapters.description: Using Gary Snyder's advice, he argues that effective action comes from love and tenderness rather than guilt, bitterness, or fighting.

chapters.title: Grief into song
chapters.description: A Gabriela Mistral poem and a line from Dina Metzger frame grief, beauty, and the possibility of transforming hurt into something luminous.

chapters.title: Finding our way
chapters.description: The Hawaiian voyaging tradition becomes an image for orientation, embodied wisdom, and what to do when we feel lost.

chapters.title: Interbeing and seeds
chapters.description: Stories of chimps, dolphins, and world-wide generosity lead into the image of carrying seeds of intention rather than trying to hold the whole world.

chapters.title: Long horizons
chapters.description: Ariyaratne's peace plan, the Dalai Lama's response, and Thomas Merton's counsel all point toward patient work without attachment to results.

chapters.title: A bodhisattva vow
chapters.description: He names the bodhisattva path and reads Diane Ackerman's vow as a public commitment to nature, healing, wonder, and peace.

chapters.title: Meditation on vastness
chapters.description: A long guided meditation expands the listener from breath and heart into a vast identity, then returns them to a personal vow and compassionate presence.

chapters.title: Blessing cords
chapters.description: He explains the symbolic cords, ties them to refuge, compassion, and intention, and has people share their vows with one another.

title: The Eco-Crisis as a Spiritual Crisis
speaker: David Loy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) There are precise and profound parallels between what Buddhism says about our usual individual predicament and our collective ecological predicament today.
podcast_description: David Loy explores the ecological crisis as a spiritual crisis, arguing that the same sense of separation that Buddhism addresses in the individual self also appears at the level of human civilization. He moves from climate change, biodiversity loss, and industrial growth to Buddhist questions about awakening, activism, and why mindfulness can become a coping strategy rather than a response to suffering. The talk closes by reading poems from Rumi and David Whyte to point toward non-separation, intimacy, and a different way of living with the earth.
short_summary: David Loy connects ecological breakdown with Buddhist teachings on self, delusion, and non-separation.

chapters.title: Fragile Earth
chapters.description: Loy opens with Earth Day framing and simple images of thin water and air to show how fragile the planet is.

chapters.title: Seeing the Iceberg
chapters.description: He introduces the two-icebergs metaphor and broadens the ecological crisis beyond climate change to fisheries, plastic, and mass extinction.

chapters.title: Reading Extinction
chapters.description: A poem by Sam Taylor brings the loss of species into a more immediate, emotional register.

chapters.title: Beyond Climate Alone
chapters.description: Loy turns from visible symptoms to the deeper causes in industrial growth society and then uses the second iceberg to question Buddhism's public face.

chapters.title: What Awakening Means
chapters.description: He contrasts escape-oriented and fit-in versions of Buddhism with a third view in which awakening deconstructs the separate self and opens compassion.

chapters.title: Growth and Alienation
chapters.description: The talk draws precise parallels between the individual sense of lack and civilization's obsession with endless growth, technology, and economic expansion.

chapters.title: Intimacy With the World
chapters.description: Loy shifts to the felt realization of non-separation through examples from nature and two poems that point toward belonging rather than isolation.

chapters.title: A Spiritual Crisis
chapters.description: He returns to the ecological crisis as a collective spiritual problem and closes by insisting that inner transformation and social transformation cannot really be separated.

title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: Earth As Witness: International Dharma Teachers' Statement on Climate Change
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley)
podcast_description: This talk weaves climate science together with Dharma teaching, beginning with the simple fact of breath and dependence on Earth’s systems. Bob Doppelt and James Baraz then move from the scale of the climate crisis to a collaboratively written statement, "Earth as Witness," that frames suffering, ethics, mindfulness, and collective action as part of the response. The talk ends by returning to hope, practical change, and a brief loving-kindness practice for the planet.
short_summary: A Dharma-informed talk on climate change that moves from the reality of interdependence to practical action, collective responsibility, and metta for the Earth.

chapters.title: Opening the conversation
chapters.description: James Baraz introduces Bob Doppelt, his climate work, and the book that connects Dharma principles with climate response.

chapters.title: Breath and interdependence
chapters.description: Bob begins by asking how we are part of the Earth's climate, using breath, oxygen, carbon, and daily life to show direct participation in Earth systems.

chapters.title: The science of warming
chapters.description: He explains greenhouse gases, the long time lag of climate effects, and why the present crisis is both serious and still responsive to action.

chapters.title: Facing the suffering
chapters.description: Bob names the fear and sorrow in the room and prepares listeners to hear the teachers' statement as something to feel, not only analyze.

chapters.title: Earth as Witness
chapters.description: He reads the climate statement from Dharma teachers, linking climate change to suffering, craving, aversion, delusion, and the need for swift reductions in emissions.

chapters.title: Dharma principles for action
chapters.description: The statement unfolds into wisdom, ethical conduct, mindfulness, collective effort, and a call to reshape institutions and daily habits.

chapters.title: Hope through visible change
chapters.description: James and Bob discuss cautious optimism, the importance of talking about concrete changes, and how shared examples can shift consciousness.

chapters.title: Individual and systemic shifts
chapters.description: Bob gives examples from home energy use, solar power, policy, corporations, and regulation to show how personal and institutional change reinforce each other.

chapters.title: Tipping points and metta
chapters.description: The talk closes with the idea that a small committed shift can matter, a reminder that suffering can become initiation, and a loving-kindness practice for the Earth.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Three)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: In this third talk of The Theatre of Selves, Rob Burbea turns from general framing to the conceptual assumptions underneath how we meet images, self, and practice. He argues that meditation and Dharma are not just about staying with what is, but about learning to notice and sometimes loosen the frameworks that shape what appears at all. The talk moves from the limits of bare attention into a more imaginal view in which figures, myths, and archetypes can be approached as active presences rather than as objects to be reduced or integrated.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how conceptual frameworks shape perception, and how imaginal practice can open a deeper, less reductive way of seeing self and world.

chapters.title: Opening the series
chapters.description: He situates the talk as a continuation of a wider reframing of Dharma, noting that some listeners may want only small techniques while the larger movement is much farther-reaching.

chapters.title: Less fabrication
chapters.description: He distinguishes simple mindfulness and staying at contact from a deeper move toward less fabrication, while stressing that one cannot live permanently in an unfabricated state.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: The talk develops the idea that imagination, perception, and self are always shaped by assumptions, and that the Dharma can be understood as skillful ways of looking that reveal emptiness.

chapters.title: Concepts underneath experience
chapters.description: He shows how conceptual frameworks operate in ordinary perception, in emotional healing, and in religious traditions, using examples from the Pali Canon, Tantric Buddhism, and modern ideas of the unconscious.

chapters.title: Images are not parts
chapters.description: Burbea contrasts the common tendency to treat images as parts of the self with another way of conceiving them as presences with their own mode of being, drawing on Corbin, Robert Duncan, and gestalt therapy as points of comparison.

chapters.title: Archetypes and autonomy
chapters.description: He develops a more imaginal stance in which figures are not to be tamed or reduced, but enlivened and respected as living presences, with Rilke used to underline their autonomy and scale.

chapters.title: What the image wants
chapters.description: Using James Hillman, he argues that images make a claim on us, bear a kind of moral and aesthetic force, and may ask for service rather than integration, with examples from teachers, monks, angels, demons, and Dionysus.

chapters.title: Myth and soulfulness
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion to myth, Buddhist tradition, and even apparently realist views, suggesting that all are ways of looking that animate experience and can restore resonance, depth, and soulfulness.

chapters.title: Cautions and closing
chapters.description: He closes by naming the dangers on both sides: imaginal inflation or escapism, and on the other side flattening Dharma into realism; the talk ends as an invitation to notice the conceptual frame and try different ones with care.

title: Climate Change, Capitalism, and Buddhadharma
speaker: Joanna Macy
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) An impassioned talk that includes a little known story from the suttas about the dangers of and suffering caused by the notion of private property. During the talk, winds of 45 mph buffeted the monastery, emergency sirens sounded in the distance, and three power outages occurred. Ends with a lively discussion with the sangha.
podcast_description: Joanna Macy begins with the climate crisis and the political economy that surrounds it, then turns to an early Buddhist origin story to ask what the Buddha might say about private property, hierarchy, and suffering. She retells the Aggañña Sutta in detail, linking craving, comparison, hoarding, and fences to the rise of violence and social order, and then brings that frame back to climate change, fossil fuel power, and collective responsibility. The talk closes in conversation with the sangha, moving toward practical action, peer support, divestment, and small groups that can keep responding together.
short_summary: Joanna Macy connects climate change, capitalism, and the Buddha’s teaching on private property to argue for collective, sangha-based climate action.

chapters.title: Opening the crisis
chapters.description: She situates the talk in the climate emergency and the Warsaw COP meeting, with a focus on the urgency, injustice, and corporate power involved.

chapters.title: Buddhist lens
chapters.description: She grounds her reading in the early Pali Canon and turns toward Buddhist ideas of anatta, mine-ness, and the suffering that comes from possession.

chapters.title: Introducing the sutta
chapters.description: She introduces the Aggañña Sutta as a satirical origin story and sets up its challenge to caste and fixed social hierarchy.

chapters.title: Desire and comparison
chapters.description: She retells the story of luminous beings, tasting, differentiation, comparison, lust, and discrimination as conditions shift.

chapters.title: Property and violence
chapters.description: The story moves to rice hoarding, fences, theft, lying, and violence, which she reads as the arising of private property and social conflict.

chapters.title: Climate and capitalism
chapters.description: She returns from the sutta to the present, arguing that climate breakdown and fossil fuel power reveal the same patterns of extraction and denial.

chapters.title: How to speak and organize
chapters.description: In the discussion, she offers ways to talk about climate without forcing resistance, and points to groups like 350.org and One Earth Sangha.

chapters.title: Divestment and resistance
chapters.description: She speaks about socially responsible investing, divestment, and the limits of international agreements in the face of corporate power.

chapters.title: Local action together
chapters.description: The closing comments return to grassroots response, peer support, and small groups, ending with the Buddha’s teaching that we go forth together for the welfare of all beings.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Two)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores imagination as a genuine part of practice, not as decoration, and keeps returning to the question of what different ways of looking make possible. He moves from the emptiness of self, through devotion, archetype, shame, and lineage, toward the sense that images can open a more soulful and less reductive way of meeting life. The talk stays practical throughout, emphasizing mindfulness of body, emotion, and relationship to the image itself.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on imaginal practice, selfhood, and how images can open a deeper, less reductive way of seeing.

chapters.title: Practice and emptiness
chapters.description: He reviews the earlier talk and frames imagination within a larger Buddhist understanding of practice aimed at seeing emptiness, not merely coping better.

chapters.title: Images in practice
chapters.description: He surveys uses of imagination in metta, samadhi, and inner-critic work, then shows how different archetypal tones shape practice.

chapters.title: Archetype and presence
chapters.description: Through Kuan Yin, Ajahn Mun, Ajahn Maha Bua, Jimi Hendrix, and Bob Dylan, he shows how figures can embody and transmit imaginal force.

chapters.title: Love and shame reframed
chapters.description: A Mary Oliver passage and several retreat stories lead into the idea that love vivifies images and can recast shame in a more healing way.

chapters.title: Self as mythic field
chapters.description: He argues that self-images are not fixed realities, but part of a web of mythic relations, lineages, and possible identities.

chapters.title: Deliberate imaginal practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes his approach from simple identity affirmation and explains how to work with images through attention to body, mood, and dialogue.

chapters.title: Relationships with images
chapters.description: He explores many possible relationships to an image, including tenderness, eroticism, and guidance, then shares vivid retreat visions of healing.

chapters.title: Iconic, not reductive
chapters.description: He contrasts narrative images with timeless, iconic ones, warns against flattening them into psychology, and closes with a retreat image of the wind of enlightenment.

chapters.title: Concepts and closing
chapters.description: He ends by stressing that assumptions and concepts are always active in practice and says he will return next time to how they open or block the path.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part One)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens a retreat series on the imagination as a serious part of Dharma practice, first situating it within emptiness, not-self, and the limits of a purely mindfulness-centered view. He then lays out several ways of seeing through self-construction, before turning to imaginal practice as a more nuanced and life-giving way to work with the body, inner criticism, devotion, and the plurality of self. The talk moves gradually from doctrinal framing into concrete examples, ending with the possibility that dialogue with images can loosen fixed identity and open new values and energies.
short_summary: An opening talk on imagination in Dharma, moving from emptiness and not-self into imaginal practice as a way to loosen identity and deepen practice.

chapters.title: Why Imagination Matters
chapters.description: Burbea frames the retreat as a long inquiry into the place of imagination in Dharma and in human life.

chapters.title: Emptiness and Not-Self
chapters.description: He sets emptiness and anatta as the core direction of practice, while distinguishing that from a narrow idea of mindfulness.

chapters.title: Seven Ways Through Self
chapters.description: He sketches seven practice approaches for seeing through self, from personality definitions to not-me contemplation.

chapters.title: Limits of Simplifying
chapters.description: He questions the view that the aggregates are the whole story, and shows why simplification can fail in romance, sexuality, and eros.

chapters.title: The Imaginal Opening
chapters.description: He names the imaginal as another way of seeing emptiness, one that does not flatten experience and can open the Dharma more widely.

chapters.title: Images in Practice
chapters.description: He gives familiar examples from metta, recollection, tantric visualization, and samadhi to show how images can support compassion, inspiration, and bodily ease.

chapters.title: Working With Inner Critics
chapters.description: He turns to the inner critic as an imaginal formation that can sometimes be deconstructed and sometimes engaged in dialogue.

chapters.title: Myths, Parents, and Voices
chapters.description: Through examples of duty, perfectionism, and heroic drive, he shows how images can carry depth beyond simple psychological labels.

chapters.title: Plurality and Dialogue
chapters.description: He closes by showing how imaginal persons can loosen a singular self and lead toward dialogue, transvaluation, and new ways of seeing.

title: Wise Responses to the Hindrances
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a practical and gentle exploration of the five hindrances, focusing less on defeating them than on changing the way they are met. He shows how sense desire, aversion, dullness, restlessness, and doubt can each be worked with through breath, posture, attention, kindness, and a wider view of practice. The talk keeps returning to a central theme: hindrances are not personal failures, and they can become a source of insight into how perception is shaped.
short_summary: A grounded teaching on the five hindrances, with practical ways to meet them and a broader view of how they reveal the shaping of perception.

chapters.title: Opening the Frame
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the five hindrances and sets up the talk as a look at practice attitude rather than just technique.

chapters.title: Not Personal Failure
chapters.description: He emphasizes that hindrances are part of being human and warns against taking them as evidence of inadequacy or lack of progress.

chapters.title: Samadhi Beyond Focus
chapters.description: The talk widens into a fuller account of samadhi as unification and harmonization, supported by kindness, openness, and letting be.

chapters.title: Working With Desire
chapters.description: Sense desire is met by returning gently, noticing pleasantness in the body, and learning to stay with subtler satisfactions.

chapters.title: Aversion and Ill Will
chapters.description: He offers ways to soften ill will by changing perception, feeling the pain inside aversion, and simplifying attention to what is actually felt.

chapters.title: Listening Underneath
chapters.description: Burbea shows how anger can carry useful energy and how boredom, resistance, and other states may be supported by aversion and deeper emotions.

chapters.title: Dullness and Energy
chapters.description: He turns to dullness and drowsiness, suggesting breath, posture, light, spaciousness, movement, and whole-body awareness as ways to brighten practice.

chapters.title: Restlessness and Subtle Effort
chapters.description: Restlessness and worry are met through the out-breath, space, and a careful tuning of effort, including the subtle forms of sinking and drifting.

chapters.title: Doubt and Perception
chapters.description: The talk closes with doubt, the value of questioning, and the larger insight that mind states color perception and can be known without believing them.

title: All In This Together
speaker: Catherine McGee
description: (Gaia House) The inter-relationship between inner and outer. Knowing that we belong as a basis for meeting and responding to the suffering of our world, both inner and outer.
podcast_description: Catherine McGee begins from Earth Care Week and uses it to open a wider question: how inner practice and outer responsibility belong to the same field. She moves through environmental examples, the experience of loneliness and resonance on retreat, and teachings on non-separation, showing how clear seeing can lead to compassionate but unforced action. The talk closes by widening into bodhisattva aspiration, with a strong emphasis on community, humility, and responding appropriately in changing conditions.
short_summary: A talk on interdependence, environmental responsibility, and how awakened practice can meet suffering without compulsion.

chapters.title: Earth Care Frame
chapters.description: Catherine opens by situating the talk in Earth Care Week and naming the larger environmental context behind the retreat theme.

chapters.title: Inner and Outer
chapters.description: She asks how Dharma practice meets overwhelming global suffering without collapsing into guilt, urgency, or helplessness.

chapters.title: We Are the Environment
chapters.description: Using John Francis as an example, she shows how restraint, silence, and sustained attention can become a direct response to ecological harm.

chapters.title: No Separation
chapters.description: She deepens the teaching that inner and outer are not truly separate, and that action affects the whole even when it seems small.

chapters.title: Resonance and Loneliness
chapters.description: A story from retreat about a bucket and the need to be seen opens into the heart-mind's longing for resonance and its capacity to turn back toward itself.

chapters.title: Emptying Out
chapters.description: She turns to the Buddha as 'awake' and to emptiness as the clearing of greed, hate, and confusion so responsiveness can arise without strain.

chapters.title: Who Hears?
chapters.description: Kuan Yin and the question 'who hears?' guide the talk into compassionate listening that can hold both inner cries and the cries of the world.

chapters.title: Community Awakening
chapters.description: She weaves in Thich Nhat Hanh, Siddhartha, and Zen teaching to describe awakening as collective, intimate, and responsive to changing conditions.

chapters.title: Bodhisattva Vow
chapters.description: The talk closes with Shantideva's aspiration to be medicine, shelter, and sustenance for beings until freedom, ending in shared sitting.

title: The Anatomy of Ignorance 2 - The Social Roots of Ignorance and climate Disruption
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) For Earth Care Week we review the three aspects of ignorance examined last week - personal (or psychological), social, and universal. We focus on the social roots of ignorance with attention mostly to understanding and responding to the climate crisis.
podcast_description: Donald Rothberg continues his "Anatomy of Ignorance" series by turning from personal and universal ignorance to the social ways a culture can fail to see clearly. He uses climate disruption as the central example, moving from scientific warnings and denial to the emotional work of taking the crisis in, and then to individual, community, and collective responses. The talk stays grounded in concrete action while also insisting that practice, compassion, and honesty are needed to meet the scale of the problem.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on social ignorance and climate disruption, moving from denial and grief to practical, collective response.

chapters.title: Reviewing the Three Ignorances
chapters.description: Rothberg recalls the previous week’s framework of personal, social, and universal ignorance and explains why naming ignorance can be sobering and hopeful at once.

chapters.title: Social Conditioning
chapters.description: He develops the social dimension of ignorance through race, gender, individualism, and the ways culture keeps larger systems out of view.

chapters.title: Climate Disruption
chapters.description: The talk turns to the climate crisis, using the IPCC report and Bill McKibben’s carbon-budget framing to show the scale of the mismatch between science and current plans.

chapters.title: How We Don’t Take It In
chapters.description: Rothberg explores how people know the facts but do not fully receive them, describing climate denial as intellectual, emotional, and tokenistic.

chapters.title: Grief and Processing
chapters.description: Drawing on Joanna Macy, he describes the need for shared settings where fear, sadness, shock, and overwhelm can be felt and transformed into energy for action.

chapters.title: Personal and Local Action
chapters.description: He moves through practical responses at the individual and institutional level, including simplicity, transportation choices, local carbon footprints, and examples such as Berkeley’s climate planning.

chapters.title: Collective Mobilization
chapters.description: Rothberg argues that technical solutions already exist, but they require a committed minority, and he presents Joanna Macy’s three-part model of preventing damage, shifting institutions, and shifting consciousness.

chapters.title: Practice and Motivation
chapters.description: He emphasizes that climate action needs Dharma resources such as compassion, equanimity, and a different motivation rooted in care for future generations.

chapters.title: Questions and Final Counsel
chapters.description: In discussion, he addresses the realities of fossil-fuel dependence, the limits of individual virtue, and the difficulty of speaking about crisis in a Dharma setting, then closes by returning to the ethical contradiction of harming one’s own children and grandchildren.

title: The Broader Context of Mindfulness
speaker: Mark Coleman
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Mindfulness is so much more that attention. Supporting wholesome qualities like patience and equanimity, Right Mindfulness specifically develops wise action in our lives and in the world.
podcast_description: Mark Coleman argues that mindfulness is more than paying attention: it sits inside the Eightfold Path and is supported by ethics, wisdom, kindness, and restraint. He uses mountain camping stories, examples from email and parenting, and reflections on military and political life to show how awareness can lead to wiser action rather than passivity. The talk closes by linking mindfulness to climate response and to the practical question of what we should do, or stop doing, once we see more clearly.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on mindfulness as ethical discernment, restraint, and wise action in everyday life and public life.

chapters.title: Mindfulness Beyond Attention
chapters.description: Mark introduces the talk by responding to the popularized, decontextualized view of mindfulness as bare attention.

chapters.title: Rooted in Tradition
chapters.description: He situates mindfulness inside the larger Buddhist training and introduces his mountain stories as a way to explore it.

chapters.title: Clarity in Action
chapters.description: A night hike and a panic attack on the mountain become examples of how presence, breath, and groundedness can help in crisis.

chapters.title: Path, Ethics, Wisdom
chapters.description: He places mindfulness within ethics, meditation, and wisdom, and shows how it reveals karma, self-talk, and the causes of suffering.

chapters.title: Unwise Attention
chapters.description: Mark explores how attention can be used manipulatively, including in advertising, status-seeking, and military contexts.

chapters.title: Qualities of Presence
chapters.description: The group reflects on what arises with mindfulness: safety, joy, cohesion, intuition, less delusion, and a lighter heart.

chapters.title: From Awareness to Conduct
chapters.description: He argues that mindfulness gathers wholesome qualities and then moves outward as speech, conduct, and non-harming action.

chapters.title: Stories of Intervention
chapters.description: A second camping story, the Buddha stopping a war, and Christopher Titmuss confronting a political leader all illustrate mindful action in the world.

chapters.title: Practice and Climate
chapters.description: Mark ends with reflections on how mindfulness informs action, including the climate crisis and Earth Care Week.

title: Practicing in Times of Big Changes
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center))
podcast_description: This talk reflects on practicing through instability, with a long opening of chant followed by a teaching on how change can be met without panic or resignation. Ayya Santacitta uses the butterfly story, the Buddha under the Bodhi tree, and dependent origination to show how clear seeing, patience, and mindfulness can meet breakdown without being overwhelmed by it. The discussion ends with a close look at feeling, grasping, and the small gap where practice can interrupt reactivity.
short_summary: Ayya Santacitta speaks about meeting personal and collective change with steadiness, clear seeing, and the practice of noticing feeling before grasping takes over.

chapters.title: Opening chant
chapters.description: The talk begins with an extended chant and settles the room before the teaching starts.

chapters.title: Facing unavoidable change
chapters.description: She turns to the reality that things will not return to an old pattern and frames practice as a support for meeting what is here.

chapters.title: Butterfly transformation
chapters.description: A caterpillar-to-butterfly story becomes an image for cultural breakdown, latent change, and the conditions for a new worldview.

chapters.title: Seeing without forcing
chapters.description: She emphasizes that change is born from looking closely and staying with what is happening rather than trying to manufacture an outcome.

chapters.title: Mara becomes flowers
chapters.description: The Buddha’s night under the Bodhi tree is used to show how not moving away from difficulty can let wisdom and compassion grow.

chapters.title: Inner and outer climate
chapters.description: She links personal practice to collective conditions, including the climate crisis, while allowing for the need to rest and turn away sometimes.

chapters.title: Paññā and insight
chapters.description: Paññā is described as the capacity to be with things as they are, and repeated clear seeing is said to mature into insight.

chapters.title: Where practice interrupts
chapters.description: The closing question turns to feeling, grasping, and the quick moment where mindfulness can stop the cycle before it becomes anger or clinging.

venue: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center

title: Follow Your Heartbreak
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) Inspired by Andrew Harvey's book A Guide to Sacred Activism. The journey of transformation, both personal and global, includes our hearts opening to all the suffering we encounter and letting our heart be shattered and break open to new possibilities. This is a natural and essential component of true awakening. This process is explored on different levels: our own dharma practice, the classical Progress of Insight and what Harvey calls "The Dark Night of the Species."
podcast_description: James Baraz reflects on a recent self-retreat and uses Andrew Harvey’s sacred activism as a frame for understanding both personal practice and the crisis of the wider world. He moves from the hardship and sweetness of retreat into the classical progress of insight, showing how suffering, fear, and disorientation can become part of awakening rather than signs of failure. The talk closes by widening back out to a collective vision: heartbreak as a condition for action, and crisis as a possible seedbed for compassion, justice, and new consciousness.
short_summary: A talk linking retreat practice, the progress of insight, and sacred activism through the theme of heartbreak as a path to awakening.

chapters.title: Opening and retreat context
chapters.description: James begins with a brief check-in about his self-retreat and the value of both solitude and practice with others.

chapters.title: Andrew Harvey's framing
chapters.description: He introduces Andrew Harvey’s book on sacred activism and explains why it has been shaping his own thinking and reading during retreat.

chapters.title: Dark night of the species
chapters.description: James uses Kali Yuga and Harvey’s language of collective decline to describe a troubled age that may also be ripe for transformation.

chapters.title: Practice through hardship
chapters.description: He turns inward, describing the ups and downs of retreat and arguing that difficulty is part of how courage, compassion, and verified faith are formed.

chapters.title: Progress of insight
chapters.description: James sketches the classical Theravada map from dissolution through fear, misery, and disgust toward equanimity and release.

chapters.title: The hero's journey
chapters.description: He connects the progress of insight with Joseph Campbell’s hero myth and examples from the Buddha, Jesus, Star Wars, and The Hunger Games.

chapters.title: Heartbreak and sacred activism
chapters.description: Returning to Andrew Harvey, James says that both personal and global transformation require a willingness to let the heart break open.

chapters.title: Signs of awakening
chapters.description: He ends with encouraging examples of global movements for justice and the possibility that consciousness can outpace fear.

title: The Dharma Of Global Climate Change
speaker: Chas DiCapua
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) How our dharma practice can turn this difficult situation onto an opportunity for practice and awakening.
podcast_description: In this talk, Chas DiCapua brings climate change into the field of Dharma practice, asking how awareness, ethics, and interdependence can shape a more useful response. He moves from the shock and avoidance that surround the issue to the habits of craving, possession, and separate-self view that help drive it, then turns toward contentment, stewardship, and collective care. The talk stays grounded in lived experience and closes with a poem that treats difficulty itself as a path of awakening.
short_summary: A Dharma inquiry into climate change, tracing the crisis to craving and separation and pointing toward contentment, interdependence, and wise response.

chapters.title: Opening the Topic
chapters.description: The talk begins by framing climate change as something to turn toward carefully, rather than avoid, and by setting up the Dharma questions that will guide the rest of the evening.

chapters.title: Smoke and Social Freeze
chapters.description: A room-smoke experiment is used to show how people can ignore clear danger when others act as if nothing is wrong, much like the cultural freeze around climate change.

chapters.title: Why It Matters
chapters.description: He explains why climate change is not just a political matter, but a moral and practical one that affects everyone, though not equally, and invites a conscious response.

chapters.title: Craving and Contentment
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Second Noble Truth, linking craving for sense pleasure to both suffering and ecological strain, then introducing Atisha's teaching on contentment.

chapters.title: Possession and Stewardship
chapters.description: He questions the assumption that land and resources can really be owned, and suggests that a stewardship view would naturally lead to less harm and more responsibility.

chapters.title: Interdependence and Story
chapters.description: Dependent arising, social narratives, and David Korten's warning about stories out of tune with reality are used to show how collective habits shape collective outcomes.

chapters.title: Cause, Effect, and Avoidance
chapters.description: Everyday examples of consumption, travel, and waste illustrate cause and effect, followed by Bob Doppelt's account of blame and avoidance and a return to the inner forces Dharma can address.

chapters.title: Mind and Separate Self
chapters.description: The Dhammapada and Ajahn Chah are used to point back to mind as the place where suffering and clarity are made, and Dilgo Khyentse Rinpoche frames ignorance and separation as root causes.

chapters.title: From Fear to Awakening
chapters.description: The ending turns toward Joanna Macy's 'great turning,' Nyoshul Khenpo's nondual language, and Jennifer Welwood's poem, presenting climate change as a demanding but potent field for awakening and compassion.

title: Planet Earth as Divine Messenger
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Marin Sangha))
podcast_description: Ayya Santacitta begins with the Buddha’s basic diagnosis of dukkha as unsatisfactoriness, then moves into the four sights and the urgency they gave to practice. From there, she widens the lens to climate change, arguing that the same discipline used to meet aging, sickness, and death is needed to face the planet’s condition without turning away. The talk closes by linking non-self, ecological identity, and practical collective action, with brief reflections from Thomas Berry, Arne Naess, and Joanna Macy.
short_summary: A Dharma talk linking the Buddha’s teaching on dukkha and impermanence to climate crisis, ecological responsibility, and practical action.

chapters.title: Arriving in the Present
chapters.description: She opens by inviting listeners to settle the body and mind, and frames practice as coming into clear contact with the present moment.

chapters.title: Dukkha and Impermanence
chapters.description: She explains the Buddha’s teaching on suffering as unsatisfactoriness, using the image of a wheel that does not quite fit and a life that is not meant to run smoothly.

chapters.title: The Four Sights
chapters.description: She retells the Buddha’s encounter with sickness, old age, death, and a truth seeker, showing how the story turned him toward renunciation.

chapters.title: Extending the Contemplation
chapters.description: She describes the monastic practice of reflecting daily on aging, illness, and death, then extends that contemplation to the planet and future generations.

chapters.title: Climate as Context
chapters.description: She argues that climate change makes the Buddha’s teaching newly urgent, because the same honesty about impermanence now applies at the scale of the Earth.

chapters.title: Home, Ecology, and Ego
chapters.description: Using the roots of ecology and economy, she suggests that we have forgotten how to care for our shared home and mistaken the larger context for something separate from ourselves.

chapters.title: Turning Toward Dissonance
chapters.description: She uses the Four Noble Truths and a reference to Philip Moffat to emphasize that healing comes from turning toward difficulty rather than escaping it.

chapters.title: Earth as Sacred Text
chapters.description: With Thomas Berry’s line about the universe as sacred scripture, she reflects on cosmic evolution, interdependence, and the need to respect what we do not know.

chapters.title: Ecological Self
chapters.description: She quotes Arne Naess on the ecological self, then connects non-self, meditation, and the social difficulty of acting on what we already know.

title: Learning from Climate Change
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery)
podcast_description: Ayya Santacitta reflects on climate change as both a practical emergency and a spiritual challenge: a situation that asks for honest attention, emotional digestion, and a wider sense of belonging. She moves from the difficulty of speaking about climate without shutting people down, to a critique of the Enlightenment-era worldview, to teachings on grief, resilience, and ecological identity. The talk closes by placing the climate crisis in a larger evolutionary frame, where discomfort can become a path into deeper responsibility and practice.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on facing climate change through honest attention, grief, and a larger ecological view of self and world.

chapters.title: Naming the dilemma
chapters.description: She opens with the challenge of teaching climate change honestly without pushing people away.

chapters.title: Shock and denial
chapters.description: Climate disruption is compared to traumatic news that first meets disbelief, anger, and shutdown.

chapters.title: Discomfort as guidance
chapters.description: She argues that discomfort carries information and can help communities adjust course.

chapters.title: Universe as scripture
chapters.description: Thomas Berry’s line becomes a way to trust the larger intelligence of evolution beyond the thinking mind.

chapters.title: Outgrown worldviews
chapters.description: She traces the Enlightenment and mechanistic thinking, suggesting they no longer fit current conditions.

chapters.title: Grief and ecological self
chapters.description: Using Arne Næss and Joanna Macy, she describes how sitting with climate grief can open a wider identity and a more natural response.

chapters.title: Attention and resilience
chapters.description: She returns to the need for samatha, clear attention, and collective resilience in the face of overwhelming information.

chapters.title: Learning from collapse
chapters.description: Easter Island and Rumi frame the danger of overshoot, while also pointing to humility, practice, and the possibility of waking up.

chapters.title: Closing appeal
chapters.description: She ends by saying the crisis can catapult us into a new worldview if we are willing to meet it and keep listening.

title: The Brahma Viharas and the Climate Challenge
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley)
podcast_description: Ayya Anandabodhi speaks about the Brahma-viharas as a practical response to climate anxiety and the wider turbulence of the world. She moves from calming the heart with loving-kindness, into compassion, appreciative joy, and equanimity, using nature images, a Buddha story, and the tension between ultimate and conventional truth. The talk ends with questions about sunflowers, monastic life, gender in the lineage, and her own path into ordination, followed by a sharing-of-blessings chant.
short_summary: A grounded talk on the Brahma-viharas as a way to meet climate grief, ethical responsibility, and overwhelm with steadiness and care.

chapters.title: Opening Homage
chapters.description: The talk begins with traditional homage to the Buddha and a quiet opening gesture of respect.

chapters.title: Practice in Turbulent Times
chapters.description: She introduces the setting, names the uncertainty of the age, and describes meditation as learning how the mind is pulled by desire, aversion, and confusion.

chapters.title: Awakening Is Human
chapters.description: She reflects on the Buddha as a human being and on awakening as a real possibility rather than something remote or exceptional.

chapters.title: Training Awareness
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to recognizing agitation, seeing states clearly, and returning again and again to presence and choice.

chapters.title: Facing Climate Anxiety
chapters.description: She turns directly to climate change, naming denial, overwhelm, and the need to let the reality in without collapsing into fear.

chapters.title: Metta and Compassion
chapters.description: A personal meditation shows how loving-kindness can steady the heart enough to open compassionately to suffering.

chapters.title: Mudita and Equanimity
chapters.description: She moves through appreciative joy and equanimity, using the beauty of Earth and the image of pelicans to evoke spacious, enduring love.

chapters.title: Ultimate and Conventional
chapters.description: She contrasts the broad, changing view with the close, frightened view, then tells the story of the Buddha caring for a sick monk to show that wisdom must include care.

chapters.title: Ethics and Renunciation
chapters.description: The talk becomes more explicit about pollution, consumption, and responsibility, and she frames the Brahma-viharas and right intention as tools for skillful action.

title: 400 and Rising
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery)
podcast_description: Ayya Anandabodhi begins with travel and community impressions from monasteries in the Pacific Northwest, then turns toward the climate crisis as a way of seeing our place in the wider web of life. From there, the talk moves through grief, responsibility, and the four Brahma Viharas as practical supports for meeting fear without numbness or despair. She closes by bringing Buddhist teachings on karma, care, and the sick monk story into a clear argument for action, tenderness, and not turning away.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on climate grief, community, and the Brahma Viharas as a grounded way to meet the planet’s crisis with care and responsibility.

chapters.title: Arriving in community
chapters.description: She opens with recent travels to monasteries and reflects on long practice, community life, and the softening that comes from sustained mutual adjustment.

chapters.title: Hermitage and the eagles
chapters.description: A visit to the Pacific Hermitage leads into images of kayaking, birds, and the felt sense of being quietly part of nature rather than separate from it.

chapters.title: The illusion of control
chapters.description: She widens into reflection on human vulnerability, modern control, and the ways city life and technological mastery can obscure our dependence on the natural world.

chapters.title: 400 parts per million
chapters.description: Using Bill McKibben and 350.org, she frames climate change in measurable terms and names 400 ppm as a serious threshold the world has already crossed.

chapters.title: Listening for the feminine
chapters.description: A brief account of hearing the Dalai Lama at an environmental summit emphasizes compassion, nurturing, listening, dialogue, and education as urgently needed qualities.

chapters.title: Seeing the larger truth
chapters.description: She brings in impermanence, no-self, and collective responsibility, asking how Buddhist practice meets the reality of climate grief and shared harm.

chapters.title: Four Brahma Viharas
chapters.description: Loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity are presented as inner supports that steady the heart without denying the crisis.

chapters.title: Equanimity and karma
chapters.description: She explains equanimity through the Buddha’s reflection on karma, emphasizing intention, responsibility, and the freedom to shape how we meet the present.

chapters.title: Care for what is sick
chapters.description: The talk ends by holding ultimate truth together with relative care, using the sick monk story to argue that impermanence never excuses neglect.

title: Reflections on the Day
speaker: Nina Wise
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: Nina Wise offers a short, embodied opening before moving into a larger reflection on climate grief, love, and collective responsibility. The talk weaves together a film about melting glaciers, a story of a prison garden, and a final image of interdependence that loosens the sense of a separate self. It ends quietly with an invitation to see what is alive, fragile, and connected right where one is.
short_summary: A performance-like Dharma reflection that moves from body awareness to climate grief, prison garden transformation, and interdependence.

chapters.title: Opening into the body
chapters.description: Nina begins with a brief, humorous introduction and a playful standing sequence that wakes up the body and the room.

chapters.title: Motion theater and release
chapters.description: She introduces motion theater, explains the form, and continues a lively series of movements, sounds, and loosening exercises.

chapters.title: From me to we
chapters.description: The talk turns from embodied play to the idea that change requires moving beyond the isolated self toward a larger communal identity.

chapters.title: Glaciers and grief
chapters.description: A viewing of Chasing Ice becomes a direct encounter with climate loss, and she follows that clear seeing into grief and love.

chapters.title: A prison garden begins
chapters.description: She tells Catherine Sneed’s story of turning a jail yard into a garden through cleanup, bureaucracy, and patient work with the men.

chapters.title: Planting and recognition
chapters.description: The story continues as a boy with a gun appears, the tree is planted, and the scene shifts from threat to a moment of shared human recognition.

chapters.title: Stardust and non-self
chapters.description: The final story links water, clouds, wind, sun, stars, and the hand, using that web of causes to point toward interdependence and the easing of 'me'.

chapters.title: Closing in the hills
chapters.description: Nina closes by imagining the drive home, the brown hills as gold, and a final attention to lupine and stars.

title: Active Hope
speaker: Joanna Macy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: Joanna Macy reflects on Active Hope as a practice of meeting ecological and social crisis without panic or paralysis. She reworks hope as something we do, then traces how uncertainty can deepen compassion, widen responsibility, and draw people into collective action. The talk moves between Bodhisattva language, personal memory, activism, grief, and a culminating reading from the Avatamsaka Sutra that expands the sense of time, body, and interconnection.
short_summary: Joanna Macy reframes hope as a practice of courageous, collective response to uncertainty and ecological crisis.

chapters.title: Opening and the book
chapters.description: The talk opens with a song reference and introduces Active Hope as the frame for the evening.

chapters.title: Hope as a verb
chapters.description: Macy explains why she no longer treats hope as wishful thinking, but as an active way of stepping forward.

chapters.title: Bodhisattva and bodhicitta
chapters.description: She defines Bodhisattva and bodhicitta as caring, interdependent intention rather than private salvation.

chapters.title: The plankton shock
chapters.description: A memory of Cousteau’s warning about plankton becomes the turning point that changes her sense of ground and purpose.

chapters.title: Panic and paralysis
chapters.description: She describes the two ditches of responding to crisis and the need to stay present without checking out.

chapters.title: Working together
chapters.description: The story of Ralph Nader’s movement and the tar sands flyover shows how action, witness, and companionship change despair.

chapters.title: Grief into action
chapters.description: A song about the tar sands leads into reflections on mourning, renewable rebuilding, and being moved by a larger purpose.

chapters.title: Service across time
chapters.description: Macy quotes a climate scientist on what service requires now and expands responsibility across generations and lifetimes.

chapters.title: One hair, all worlds
chapters.description: She ends with the Avatamsaka Sutra’s vision of interpenetration, where one body, one moment, and one place contain the whole web of life.

title: Connecting Inner and Outer Responses to Climate Change
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) At this time of climate disruption, we need powerful responses--integrating more "inner" spiritual practices and principles, on the one hand, with skill in "outer" responses, on the other. This integration or marriage can happen in many ways as we participate in the "great turning"--whether our primary emphasis, to use Joanna Macy's analysis, is stopping further damage from occurring, transforming our institutions, or helping to shift consciousness. Without this integration, however, spiritual practice runs the risk of becoming a kind of middle-class escapism and activism runs the risk of being caught in self-righteousness, attachment to views, demonization of the "enemy," and burnout. We need a new integration! We look at several dharma principles that can be the basis for such an integration, consider briefly how Spirit Rock is responding (and might respond further) to climate issues, and especially look at the figure of the bodhisattva.
podcast_description: Donald Rothberg offers a grounded talk on climate change as a call to join inner practice with outer response. He moves from a short meditation invocation into a critique of spiritual escapism and reactive activism, then turns to the bodhisattva ideal as a model for sustained, compassionate engagement. The talk ends with practical reflections on personal, communal, and collective action, and with brief readings that hold urgency and tenderness together.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on climate change that argues for joining meditation, ethics, and collective action through the bodhisattva path.

chapters.title: Opening Invocation
chapters.description: Rothberg begins with a short meditation on spacious, steady awareness and uses it to dissolve the split between inner practice and the earth.

chapters.title: Why Integration Matters
chapters.description: He lays out the central theme: without connecting practice and action, Dharma can become escapism and activism can become reactive or self-righteous.

chapters.title: The Bodhisattva Ideal
chapters.description: Drawing on bodhisattva vows across traditions, he presents the bodhisattva as the figure who can hold awakening and service together.

chapters.title: Finding Your Calling
chapters.description: Rothberg uses Howard Thurman and Joanna Macy to suggest that people have different roles in the great turning and should find the work that makes them come alive.

chapters.title: Mindfulness Must Act
chapters.description: He quotes Thich Nhat Hanh to show engaged Buddhism as a decision to practice and help at the same time, especially in the face of suffering.

chapters.title: Dharma and Climate Practice
chapters.description: The talk turns to ethics, wisdom, and the need to widen practice beyond the individual into relational and collective life, including concrete examples from Spirit Rock and Berkeley.

chapters.title: Personal to Systemic Response
chapters.description: He moves through household changes, transportation, and larger climate strategies, arguing that personal discipline matters most when it supports systemic change.

chapters.title: Reflective Exercise
chapters.description: Rothberg invites listeners to name what calls them now, then asks them to share with a neighbor where they feel drawn to respond.

chapters.title: Closing Readings
chapters.description: He ends with short readings from Rabbi Tarfon, a Zen teacher, and Deena Metzger, emphasizing steady work, ordinary practice, and love in urgent times.

title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: From Me to We
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)
podcast_description: This Earth Day talk links climate change to core Dharma themes: interdependence, karma, moral conduct, trusteeship, and the freedom to choose differently. James Baraz opens by framing the gathering as sangha-based refuge and a call for teacher leadership, then Bob Doppelt offers a practical framework for responding to climate breakdown without being driven by fear. The talk includes a short guided reflection on breathing, the living systems that sustain it, and how awareness can widen into responsibility.
short_summary: A Dharma-centered response to climate change that moves from sangha, to interdependence, to five practical commitments for ethical action.

chapters.title: Opening the gathering
chapters.description: The talk begins with the Earth Day setting, the climate focus, and the introduction of James Baraz and Bob Doppelt.

chapters.title: Sangha and teacher request
